proposals.title: A Wireless, Injectable Mesh Electronics Platform with a Remotely Located Power Source for Chronic Neural Recording
proposals.motivation: Current minimally invasive neural probes, such as Lieber's ultraflexible endovascular or injectable mesh electronics, integrate power sources or bulky tethers, which limit long-term biocompatibility and chronic recording stability. Decoupling the power source from the recording device would drastically reduce the foreign body mass implanted in delicate neural tissue, mitigating chronic immune response and enabling truly stable, long-term interfaces. This is a critical step towards realizing permanent, high-fidelity brain-computer interfaces for fundamental neuroscience and clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a nanowire-based mesh electronic neural recorder can be powered wirelessly via inductive coupling from a physically separate, subcutaneously implanted millimeter-scale power module, without compromising signal fidelity or device biointegration. This decoupled architecture will significantly improve the chronic recording stability and biocompatibility compared to integrated power systems.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible, syringe-injectable mesh electronics featuring high-density platinum-nanowire recording electrodes and a microfabricated silicon-nanowire (SiNW) RF receiver coil. A separate, encapsulable power module containing a millimeter-scale transmitter coil and a microscale battery will be designed for subcutaneous implantation. We will employ frequency-selective, magnetically resonant inductive coupling at ~13.56 MHz to efficiently transfer power across several millimeters of tissue. The system will be validated in vivo in rodent models, comparing signal-to-noise ratio and chronic immune response (via histology) against tethered and integrated-battery controls over 6-month periods.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for powering next-generation bioelectronic implants, directly addressing the chronic stability challenge. It will deliver a fully characterized, wireless platform for chronic, high-bandwidth neural recording with minimal implanted hardware. The decoupled design principles will be broadly applicable to other implantable devices for stimulation, sensing, and drug delivery.
proposals.novelty: While wireless power for implants exists, applying it to fully injectable, sub-micron-thick mesh electronics that interpenetrate neural tissue is unprecedented. The novelty lies in the co-design of a nanoscale receiver integrated into the mesh with a remote, minimally invasive power module, creating a system where no rigid or voluminous components reside at the precise recording site.

proposals.title: Atomic Layer Deposition of Conformal p-i-n Junctions in Coaxial Nanowires for High-Density, Low-Impedance Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: Current neural probes based on planar silicon or polymer substrates face a fundamental trade-off between high-density recording and low electrochemical impedance, which limits signal-to-noise ratio and long-term stability. Nanowire-based probes offer a promising path to miniaturization, but existing methods for creating functional semiconductor junctions within their complex 3D geometries lack the uniformity and thickness control needed for optimal electronic and ionic transport. A novel fabrication method is required to realize the full potential of nanowire electronics for chronic brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that atomic layer deposition (ALD) can be used to synthesize ultra-thin, conformal, and precisely doped p-i-n semiconductor junctions within the annular structure of coaxial nanowires, thereby creating an ideal platform for low-impedance, multiplexed recording sites at cellular scales.
proposals.method: We will fabricate freestanding silicon nanowire cores via vapor-liquid-solid growth. Using a custom ALD reactor, we will sequentially deposit conformal, nanoscale layers of p-type (e.g., boron-doped amorphous silicon), intrinsic, and n-type (e.g., phosphorus-doped amorphous silicon) semiconductors to form the coaxial p-i-n junction. Precise control over doping concentration and layer thickness (targeting sub-20nm total) will be achieved by modulating precursor pulse sequences and cycle numbers. The electronic properties and junction quality will be characterized via in-situ electrical probing during fabrication and ex-situ IV measurements, followed by integration into flexible polyimide mesh probes for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and neural recording tests in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable, bottom-up nanofabrication route for creating high-performance semiconductor junctions in 3D neural probe geometries. It will directly enable a new class of ultraflexible, injectable probes with recording site densities and impedance characteristics surpassing current state-of-the-art planar and nanowire devices, advancing the goal of stable, high-bandwidth brain-computer interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While ALD is known for conformal coatings and nanowires are established neural platforms, the application of ALD to create sequentially doped, multi-layer semiconductor junctions *within* the coaxial architecture of a single nanowire is a novel synthesis strategy. This moves beyond simple ALD insulation or surface functionalization to engineer the core electronic functionality of the nanodevice itself.

proposals.title: Microfluidic Assembly of Large-Scale, Ordered Nanowire Arrays for High-Efficiency, Injectable Photovoltaic Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The development of chronically stable, high-bandwidth neural interfaces requires miniaturized, flexible, and biocompatible devices with integrated power sources. Pre-2026 work in Lieber's group has pioneered ultraflexible, injectable electronic probes, but powering such devices often relies on percutaneous tethers or bulky batteries, limiting long-term utility. Scalable fabrication of ordered, high-density semiconductor nanowire arrays, which are excellent for both recording and photovoltaic energy conversion, remains a significant challenge for creating fully autonomous bioelectronic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that microfluidic directed assembly, leveraging principles from colloidal science and fluid dynamics, can be used to create wafer-scale, defect-tolerant arrays of compositionally modulated nanowires with the spatial order and electronic alignment necessary for efficient photovoltaic energy harvesting within a flexible, biocompatible substrate.
proposals.method: We will adapt microfluidic channel designs with herringbone or pillar-based traps to direct the assembly of pre-synthesized, p-type/intrinsic/n-type (PIN) silicon nanowires from solution. A combination of surface functionalization, flow rate control, and dielectrophoretic alignment will be used to achieve high-density, uniaxial ordering. The assembled arrays will be transferred to a biocompatible, flexible polymer (e.g., SU-8 or polyimide) and interconnected via lithographically defined transparent electrodes (ITO/PEDOT:PSS). Device performance will be characterized by current-voltage measurements under simulated biological tissue optical conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable, bottom-up manufacturing pathway for large-area nanowire photovoltaic arrays directly integrable with neural probes. It will provide a foundational power technology for next-generation, untethered bioelectronic implants. The methodology could extend beyond photovoltaics to the ordered assembly of nanowire-based sensors and stimulators for multifunctional neural interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics has been used for particle sorting, its application for creating electrically connected, large-scale ordered arrays of complex semiconductor nanowires for implantable photovoltaics is novel. This merges Lieber's expertise in nanowire neural devices with scalable assembly techniques distinct from traditional top-down lithography or stochastic deposition methods.

proposals.title: Self-Powered, Injectable Nanowire Mesh Probes for Simultaneous Optogenetic Stimulation and Neural Recording
proposals.motivation: A major challenge for chronic, minimally invasive brain-machine interfaces is the need for tethered power supplies and separate devices for stimulation and recording, which increase tissue damage and limit free behavior. Pre-2026 work on ultraflexible, injectable mesh electronics demonstrates stable long-term neural recording, but active stimulation still relies on external wired power. Integrating energy harvesting directly into the probe would enable fully autonomous, closed-loop neuromodulation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core-shell silicon nanowire architecture can be engineered to function simultaneously as a high-efficiency photovoltaic cell for harvesting near-infrared light and as a low-noise field-effect transistor for recording neural activity, creating a single, dual-function device element.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh probes via deterministic nanomaterial assembly, using p-type/intrinsic/n-type (PIN) doped silicon nanowires as the core active element. The PIN junction will enable photovoltaic conversion of transcranially delivered 850-950 nm light. The same nanowire will be integrated into a transistor configuration, where the photogenerated voltage will gate the channel conductance, simultaneously powering the device and transducing local field potentials. Probes will be injected into rodent cortex for validation, using pulsed NIR light for optogenetic stimulation while recording evoked and spontaneous activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first injectable neural probe that is both self-powered and capable of simultaneous optical stimulation and electrical recording. It will establish a new paradigm for closed-loop, wireless neuromodulation, significantly reducing the system complexity and tethering constraints of current brain interfaces. The fundamental device physics will advance the integration of energy harvesting and sensing in flexible bioelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While nanowire photodetectors and photovoltaics exist, their monolithic integration into a single nanostructure that performs both power generation and biosensing for neural applications is unexplored. This moves beyond using separate photovoltaic modules to power discrete sensors, creating an intrinsically dual-use nanoscale building block.

proposals.title: Synthesis of Ultra-Short, Isotropic Nanowire 'Neurodots' for High-Density Injectable Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Current ultraflexible and injectable neural probes, such as mesh electronics and endovascular probes pioneered by Lieber's group, rely on linear nanowires with high aspect ratios. While enabling remarkable tissue integration, their filamentous geometry can limit the ultimate density of recording or stimulation sites within a confined injection volume. Synthesizing nanoscale building blocks with dimensions comparable in all axes (i.e., 'dots' or short rods) could enable denser, more isotropic packing for enhanced spatial resolution in chronic brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by precisely controlling the vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth kinetics and catalyst deactivation, we can synthesize silicon nanowire segments with lengths deliberately limited to 50-200 nm, comparable to their diameters, creating a new class of quasi-zero-dimensional, electronically active nanostructures suitable for ultra-dense neural interfaces.
proposals.method: We will adapt a standard chemical vapor deposition (CVD) VLS process using gold nanocluster catalysts and silane precursor. Growth termination will be achieved through rapid, pulsed precursor delivery and abrupt switching to an inert carrier gas, coupled with rapid cooling. Alternatively, we will explore in-situ catalyst poisoning via timed introduction of a sulfur-containing compound (e.g., H2S) to selectively deactivate the catalytic alloy. The morphology, crystallinity, and surface chemistry of the resulting 'neurodots' will be characterized via TEM, SEM, and XPS.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational materials synthesis platform for isotropic, active nanoscale components. It will provide the core building block for a next generation of injectable neural probes with potentially orders-of-magnitude higher site density per volume. It directly extends the Lieber group's expertise in nanowire synthesis toward a new paradigm for 3D integrated neural electronics.
proposals.novelty: While VLS is a mature technique for long nanowires, its deliberate use to create ultra-short, monodisperse segments with retained single-crystallinity and electronic properties for neural interfacing is unexplored. This represents a materials-centric innovation complementary to existing device integration strategies.

proposals.title: Sequential Pulsed-Laser Ablation for the VLS Synthesis of Multifunctional Core-Shell Nanowire Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: The development of chronic, stable, and multifunctional neural interfaces requires probes with nanoscale feature sizes, high-density multisite recording, and integrated stimulation capabilities. Pre-2026 work, including Lieber's ultraflexible mesh electronics, demonstrates the need for next-generation materials that combine these functionalities within a single, minimally invasive device. Current synthesis methods for complex nanowire heterostructures often lack the spatial control and material versatility needed for advanced bioelectronic applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Sequential pulsed-laser ablation of different target materials into a vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth chamber will enable the precise, layer-by-layer deposition of functional shells (e.g., conductive, insulating, or piezoelectric) around a semiconductor core nanowire, creating a new class of multifunctional neural probes.
proposals.method: We will employ a custom ultra-high vacuum chamber equipped with multiple, independently rotatable targets for sequential ablation by a pulsed excimer laser (KrF, 248 nm). A silicon nanowire core will be grown via the Au-catalyzed VLS mechanism. Subsequently, without breaking vacuum, we will ablate a second target (e.g., platinum or a piezoelectric material like ZnO) to deposit a conformal shell. The timing, laser fluence, and background gas pressure for each ablation step will be precisely controlled to govern shell thickness and crystallinity. The resulting core-shell nanowires will be integrated into flexible mesh electronics using established 'stitching' techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials synthesis platform for creating complex nanowire heterostructures with tailored electronic and electromechanical properties. It will directly contribute to the fabrication of next-generation neural probes capable of simultaneous high-fidelity recording, low-impedance stimulation, and potentially mechanical sensing, all from a single nanowire building block.
proposals.novelty: While core-shell nanowires exist, their synthesis via sequential, in-situ pulsed-laser ablation within a VLS process is unexplored. This method offers unique advantages in combining materials with vastly different properties (e.g., semiconductors and metals) that are difficult to co-deposit using conventional chemical vapor deposition.

proposals.title: Scalable, Electrically Pumped Nanowire VCSEL Arrays for High-Density, Low-Heat Neural Interfacing
proposals.motivation: Current optical neural interfaces using external lasers and fiber optics are bulky and limit chronic, freely-behaving studies. Electrically pumped, microscale light sources integrated directly into neural tissue are needed for scalable optogenetics. Vertical-cavity surface-emitting lasers (VCSELs) offer ideal beam profiles and low-power operation, but conventional planar VCSELs are difficult to scale down and integrate onto flexible, biocompatible substrates for deep-brain interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: That vertically aligned III-V semiconductor nanowire arrays can be monolithically integrated on silicon to form electrically injected, single-mode VCSELs, and that their nanoscale dimensions and vertical emission will enable dense integration into ultraflexible electronic neural probes for localized optical stimulation.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ordered arrays of n-type InGaAs/InP core-shell nanowires via vapor-liquid-solid growth on silicon substrates. A conformal dielectric DBR will be deposited around the nanowires to form the bottom mirror, with a top dielectric DBR deposited after p-contact formation to complete the cavity. Precise doping and heterostructure design within the nanowire will facilitate efficient carrier injection and optical confinement. These nanowire VCSEL arrays will be heterogeneously integrated with Lieber-group's existing flexible mesh electronics via a 'stitching' transfer process, enabling simultaneous electrical recording and optical stimulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first demonstration of an electrically pumped nanowire-based VCSEL, a critical milestone for integrated photonics. It will provide a novel device platform for minimally invasive, multifunctional neural probes that combine high-density electrical recording with cell-type-specific optical stimulation in a single, injectable form factor, advancing the study of neural circuits.
proposals.novelty: While nanowire LEDs exist, an electrically pumped nanowire VCSEL has not been achieved. The approach uniquely combines bottom-up nanowire synthesis for material versatility with top-down processing for scalable device integration, targeting a specific application in next-generation brain-machine interfaces.

proposals.title: Amorphous and Polycrystalline Silicon Nanowires for Scalable, High-Density Neural Mesh Electronics
proposals.motivation: Bottom-up synthesized single-crystalline nanowires (NWs) have enabled revolutionary ultraflexible neural probes, but their synthesis is complex, low-yield, and expensive, hindering scalable manufacturing. Amorphous (a-Si) and polycrystalline (poly-Si) silicon thin films are the backbone of scalable microelectronics and can be patterned into NW geometries. It is unknown whether these scalable, top-down materials can meet the stringent electrical and mechanical requirements for high-fidelity, chronically stable brain interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a-Si and poly-Si nanowires, fabricated via top-down lithographic patterning and etching of deposited thin films, can achieve the necessary electrical performance (impedance, signal-to-noise ratio) and mechanical compliance for chronic neural recording and stimulation, without requiring single-crystalline perfection.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh electronics using established thin-film processes: depositing a-Si and poly-Si layers on sacrificial substrates, patterning them via electron-beam lithography into sub-100 nm nanowire networks, and metallizing interconnects. Devices will be released and assembled into 3D macroporous meshes via syringe injection. We will systematically characterize their electronic properties (IV curves, impedance spectroscopy), mechanical flexibility (cyclic bending tests), and electrochemical stability. Finally, we will benchmark their neural recording performance in vitro (acute brain slices) and in vivo in rodent models against established single-crystalline Si NW controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials roadmap for transitioning neural mesh electronics from bespoke, bottom-up synthesis to scalable, top-down semiconductor manufacturing. It will provide the first direct performance comparison of crystalline vs. non-crystalline nanowires in bio-interfaces, potentially unlocking high-volume production of next-generation brain probes.
proposals.novelty: While the field assumes single-crystallinity is essential for nanoscale electronic performance in flexible bio-interfaces, we challenge this paradigm by leveraging the proven scalability and tunability of amorphous and polycrystalline silicon, materials previously unexplored for injectable, free-standing neural meshes.

proposals.title: Bottom-Up Synthesis of a Molecular-Wire Neural Mesh for Subcellular Recording and Stimulation
proposals.motivation: Current ultraflexible neural probes, while revolutionary, are fabricated top-down, limiting their ultimate feature size and seamless integration with neural tissue at the subcellular level. A bottom-up approach using molecular-scale components could enable the creation of neural interfaces with unprecedented spatial resolution and biomimetic mechanical properties, potentially allowing for chronic, stable recording from individual neuronal compartments and synapses. This addresses a core challenge in neuroscience: understanding neural computation at its fundamental scale with minimal tissue disruption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and conjugated organic molecules can be assembled into a freestanding, electrically conductive mesh that functions as a scalable, injectable neural interface capable of recording and stimulating activity at subcellular resolutions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a hybrid molecular network using solution-based self-assembly of SWCNTs and thiol-terminated oligothiophene wires, leveraging π-π stacking and covalent cross-linking to form a porous, flexible mesh. This mesh will be functionalized with lipid bilayers and cell-adhesion peptides to promote neural integration. We will develop a capillary-based injection protocol, similar to Lieber's prior work, to deliver the mesh into rodent cortex. Electrical characterization (impedance, charge injection capacity) and multiplexed recording/stimulation will be performed using a custom high-channel-count system, with validation via simultaneous two-photon calcium imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a fully bottom-up, molecular-scale neural interface, pushing the physical limits of bioelectronic device miniaturization. It will provide a new platform to investigate subcellular and synaptic electrophysiology in vivo with minimal glial scarring. The project will also contribute novel methods for the directed assembly and in vivo deployment of molecular electronic networks.
proposals.novelty: While top-down nanowire probes exist, the direct use of individual SWCNTs and synthetic molecular wires as the primary building blocks for an implantable, functional neural recorder is unexplored. This approach moves beyond semiconductor nanofabrication to a chemical paradigm for neural interface construction.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Characterization of Radial Core-Shell Nanowire Superlattices for Multimodal Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work in Lieber's group has pioneered axial nanowire superlattices, enabling multifunctional probes with sequential segments of distinct materials for recording, stimulation, and drug delivery. However, axial modulation limits the density of functional elements per unit length and constrains the probe's cross-sectional geometry. A radial architecture could integrate multiple materials and functions simultaneously around a single nanowire core, enabling more compact, high-bandwidth neural interfaces for minimally invasive brain recording.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the superlattice growth direction from axial to radial, we can synthesize single-nanowire probes with a conductive core and concentric, epitaxial shells of distinct semiconductor and insulating materials, thereby creating a multimodal recording and stimulation device with a minimized cross-sectional footprint.
proposals.method: We will adapt a vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth process using a gold nanoparticle catalyst to first grow a silicon nanowire core. Subsequently, using a sequential chemical vapor deposition (CVD) approach with precise gas-phase precursor switching, we will epitaxially deposit concentric shells of materials such as silicon (for intrinsic recording), silicon-germanium (for tuned conductivity), and silicon dioxide (for insulation). Growth parameters (temperature, pressure, precursor ratios) will be systematically varied to control shell thickness, crystallinity, and interface quality. The resulting radial superlattice nanowires will be characterized via TEM, EDX, and electrical probing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials platform for neural probe design, enabling the integration of multiple electronic and ionic functions into a single, sub-micron diameter wire. It directly contributes to the goal of creating ultraflexible, high-density, and multifunctional biointerfaces for the brain, potentially allowing simultaneous electrical recording, optical stimulation, and biochemical sensing from a single implantable device.
proposals.novelty: While axial superlattices are well-established, the synthesis of high-quality, epitaxial radial core-shell superlattices in semiconductor nanowires for neural applications is unexplored. This inversion of the modulation direction represents a fundamental shift in nanomaterial design for bioelectronics, moving from a linear, segmented architecture to a concentric, integrated one.

proposals.title: Injectable 3D Nanowire Superlattice Scaffolds as Programmable, Multimodal Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neural interfaces, including ultraflexible probes and injectable meshes, face a fundamental trade-off between high-density, multimodal recording/stimulation and minimal chronic tissue damage. Existing 3D scaffolds lack the intrinsic electronic and topological complexity needed for seamless biointegration and distributed computation. Assembling synthesized nanowire superlattices—which exhibit tunable electronic, optical, and mechanical properties—into 3D bulk metamaterials could bridge this gap, creating a new class of synthetic neural tissue.
proposals.hypothesis: That freestanding 3D bulk materials assembled from pre-synthesized semiconductor nanowire superlattices can be engineered to exhibit simultaneously programmable electronic conductivity, optical transparency, and tissue-like mechanical compliance, enabling their injection and stable integration as active, multifunctional neural interfaces.
proposals.method: We will synthesize p-type/intrinsic/n-type silicon and III-V nanowire superlattices via vapor-liquid-solid growth. These will be functionalized with peptides to promote self-assembly into porous 3D macrostructures using a controlled ice-templating (freeze-casting) technique. The bulk metamaterial's electrical connectivity will be established through post-assembly annealing and conductive polymer infiltration (e.g., PEDOT:PSS). We will characterize their impedance, charge injection capacity, and stiffness, then evaluate functionality via acute optical stimulation and electrical recording in ex vivo brain slices, followed by injection and chronic assessment in rodent cortex.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for neural interface fabrication, moving from 2D device implantation to the in situ formation of 3D active tissue scaffolds. It will provide the first demonstration of a bulk electronic metamaterial whose properties emerge from the designed assembly of nanoscale superlattice blocks, directly contributing to the fields of neuroengineering and bottom-up nanomaterial synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While Lieber's prior work stitches 2D macroporous nets or linear probes into tissue, this proposal uniquely uses pre-fabricated nanowire superlattices as tunable 'atoms' to construct 3D bulk metamaterials with emergent electronic properties, creating an injectable, freestanding electronic tissue rather than a pre-formed device.

proposals.title: Real-Time, In-Situ Assembly of Nanowire Neural Probes via Programmable Dielectrophoretic Trapping
proposals.motivation: A major challenge in creating next-generation, minimally invasive brain-machine interfaces is the precise, three-dimensional integration of nanoscale recording elements into soft, dynamic neural tissue. Existing methods, including pre-fabricated flexible mesh electronics, require surgical implantation. A method for assembling high-density, functional probes directly within the brain's extracellular space or vasculature would enable seamless integration and access to previously unreachable neural circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dielectrophoretic (DEP) forces, generated by microfabricated electrode arrays, can be used to dynamically trap, align, and position semiconducting nanowires from a biocompatible carrier solution in real-time, forming the conductive backbone of a neural recording probe directly at the target site.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a dual-function microfluidic-DEP device with an array of addressable Ti/Au microelectrodes. Silicon nanowires, synthesized and characterized pre-implantation, will be suspended in an isotonic solution. Applying specific AC field parameters (frequency 100 kHz – 10 MHz, voltage 1-10 Vpp) to selected electrodes will create DEP traps. We will develop a closed-loop control algorithm that uses real-time impedance monitoring at the electrodes to detect nanowire capture and guide their sequential assembly into predefined multi-electrode geometries, such as linear arrays or branching structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational method for the in-situ synthesis of neural probes, moving beyond pre-fabricated implantation. It will provide a new toolkit for creating customizable, three-dimensional recording arrays within delicate brain regions, such as the hippocampus or cortical layers, via intravascular or parenchymal delivery. This directly advances the goal of seamless, minimally disruptive bio-integrated electronics.
proposals.novelty: While DEP is known for nanoparticle manipulation in vitro, its application for the real-time, programmable assembly of functional neural devices inside biological tissue or vasculature is unexplored. This merges the concepts of injectable electronics and active nanoscale control to create a dynamic fabrication platform.

proposals.title: Avidity-Engineered Nanowire Probes for Robust, Long-Term Neurochemical Sensing
proposals.motivation: Current implantable neural probes for neurochemical sensing rely on surface-immobilized enzymes or antibodies, which can degrade or foul in vivo, limiting long-term stability and signal fidelity. This is a critical barrier for chronic brain-computer interfaces and studies of neurochemical dynamics. A new paradigm for creating stable, specific sensor interfaces without fragile, high-affinity biological receptors is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dense, organized array of low-affinity, synthetic peptide motifs on a nanowire field-effect transistor (FET) surface will enable specific, stable detection of target neurochemicals through cooperative multivalent binding, mimicking the avidity effects seen in biological systems.
proposals.method: We will functionalize ultraflexible Lieber-style silicon nanowire FET probes with dense monolayers of engineered, redox-insensitive peptide loops designed for weak, reversible binding to target moieties (e.g., the catechol group of dopamine). Using molecular dynamics simulations pre-2026 (e.g., GROMACS), we will model and optimize peptide surface density and conformation for cooperative analyte capture. The binding-induced avidity effect will transduce a measurable change in nanowire conductance, which we will calibrate in vitro against traditional fast-scan cyclic voltammetry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for robust biochemical sensing interfaces in neural electronics, moving beyond reliance on canonical high-affinity binders. It will deliver a prototype chronic dopamine sensor with improved resistance to biofouling and degradation, directly addressing a key limitation in modern neurotechnology.
proposals.novelty: While multivalency is known in bioconjugation chemistry, its deliberate application to create specificity from low-affinity elements on implantable electronic neural probes is unexplored. This merges concepts from structural biology with nanoelectronics in a novel way for neural interface design.

proposals.title: Engineered Disorder in Solution-Processed ZnO Nanowire Meshes for Ultrasensitive, Multimodal Neural and Biochemical Sensing
proposals.motivation: High-performance neural and tissue-interfacing sensors, such as ultraflexible nanowire probes, are often constrained by the cost and complexity of silicon-based, crystalline fabrication. For widespread deployment in distributed neural monitoring or chronic biomedical implants, there is a critical need for low-cost, scalable platforms that do not sacrifice functionality. This proposal addresses this by turning a major limitation of scalable processes—structural disorder—into a design principle for enhanced sensing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetically controlled amorphous phases and grain boundary networks in low-temperature, solution-processed metal oxide nanowires can be engineered to create beneficial electronic trap states and adsorption sites, leading to enhanced sensitivity and new multimodal sensing capabilities for neurotransmitters and metabolic byproducts.
proposals.method: We will fabricate meshes of zinc oxide (ZnO) nanowires via low-temperature hydrothermal and electrospinning techniques, explicitly varying precursor chemistry and annealing parameters to control amorphous content and grain boundary density. These nanowire networks will be integrated onto flexible polymeric substrates using Lieber Group's 'stitching' assembly. Sensor performance will be characterized by correlating structural analysis (TEM, XRD) with electronic measurements (IV, impedance spectroscopy) and in vitro sensitivity to target analytes (dopamine, lactate, pH) in simulated interstitial fluid.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new fabrication paradigm where disorder is a tunable material property, not a defect, for sensor design. It will yield a foundational platform for ultra-low-cost, multifunctional neural interfaces that can be deployed at scale. The results will provide a roadmap for applying disorder engineering to other non-silicon material systems for biomedical sensing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses nanowires despite their imperfections, this project actively designs and utilizes disorder as the primary functional element. It merges the concepts of scalable, disordered material synthesis with the high-performance neural interface design principles of the Lieber Group, a previously unexplored synergy.

proposals.title: Scalable, Injectable Neural Probes from Solution-Processed and Polycrystalline Semiconductor Nanowires
proposals.motivation: The Lieber group's pioneering work on ultraflexible, injectable nanowire mesh electronics has demonstrated transformative potential for chronic, stable neural recording. However, the reliance on pristine, single-crystalline silicon nanowires (SiNWs) necessitates complex, high-temperature, and low-yield bottom-up synthesis (e.g., VLS growth), which presents a significant barrier to scalable, low-cost manufacturing and widespread adoption in large-scale neuroscience or clinical applications. Exploring alternative nanomaterials that are compatible with high-throughput, solution-based fabrication could democratize access to this powerful neurotechnology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solution-processed semiconductor nanowires, including polycrystalline silicon or compound semiconductors (e.g., InAs, InP), can be engineered into ultraflexible mesh probes that retain the critical electrophysiological recording performance of pristine SiNWs, while enabling scalable, low-cost manufacturing via printing or spin-coating techniques.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh electronics using nanowires synthesized via solution-based, catalyst-free methods (e.g., colloidal synthesis for III-V materials or laser ablation for poly-Si). These nanowires will be transferred and patterned into FET-based recording devices using pre-2026 microfluidic printing or spin-coating onto sacrificial layers, followed by photolithographic definition of metallic interconnects (Cr/Au). The electronic and mechanical properties (carrier mobility, flexibility, strain tolerance) will be characterized. Finally, the probes will be injected into rodent cortex for acute and chronic (≥ 4 weeks) validation of single-unit recording fidelity, signal-to-noise ratio, and biocompatibility, directly comparing performance to established pristine SiNW probes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials toolkit for scalable neural interface fabrication, directly addressing a major translational bottleneck. It will provide the first systematic study of how nanomaterial crystallinity and synthesis route impact chronic recording performance in flexible mesh electronics, guiding future manufacturing choices.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used non-silicon nanomaterials for rigid probes, applying solution-processed, potentially imperfect nanowires to the specific, demanding architecture of injectable, ultraflexible mesh electronics—where mechanical integrity and electronic stability are paramount—is unexplored and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Injectable Nanowire Mesh Probes for In Vivo Neurochemical Sensing via Biomolecule-Programmed Nanomechanical Gating
proposals.motivation: Current methods for monitoring neurochemical dynamics in vivo, such as microdialysis or fluorescent biosensors, often lack the spatiotemporal resolution, long-term stability, or molecular specificity needed to decode complex signaling. A label-free, direct electronic readout platform that translates specific biomolecular binding into a robust electrical signal would enable chronic, high-fidelity recording of neurotransmitters and neuromodulators in freely behaving animals, a critical unmet need in neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a freestanding, flexible nanowire mesh functionalized with target-specific receptors can act as a nanomechanical gate, where ligand binding induces a quantifiable, reversible change in the mesh's electrical impedance, providing a label-free, amplificatory signal for specific neurochemical detection.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible, injectable mesh probes from silicon nanowires, leveraging Lieber group's established nanofabrication and syringe-injection techniques. Specific neurotransmitter receptors (e.g., for dopamine or glutamate) will be conjugated to nanowire junctions. Binding-induced conformational changes in the receptor will mechanically modulate the junction conductance. We will develop a custom low-noise amplifier and signal processing algorithm to isolate the binding-correlated impedance shifts from background electrophysiological activity, enabling real-time, multiplexed chemical sensing.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of chronically stable, label-free biosensors for in vivo neurochemistry, directly integrating chemical sensing with existing electrophysiological recording capabilities on neural probes. It will establish a generalizable platform where biomolecular recognition itself becomes the active transducer element, moving beyond indirect optical or enzymatic detection schemes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct engineering of the biomolecular binding event to mechanically gate a nanoscale electronic circuit in vivo, creating intrinsic signal amplification without secondary labels or reporters, which is distinct from existing field-effect or electrochemical biosensors.

proposals.title: Temporally Multiplexed Neurochemical Sensing via Cyclic Enzymatic Labeling and Erasure on Nanowire Probes
proposals.motivation: Understanding brain function requires multiplexed, long-term recording of neurochemicals, but existing in vivo probes struggle to combine high multiplexity with minimal tissue damage and simple electronics. Current approaches using multiple distinct sensors or spectral multiplexing increase probe footprint and electronic complexity, conflicting with the drive towards ultraflexible, minimally invasive interfaces. A new paradigm is needed to achieve high-dimensional chemical sensing from a single, simple readout channel within a compact device.
proposals.hypothesis: That multiplexed neurochemical detection can be achieved with a single, simple electronic readout by transforming the problem from one of simultaneous spectral/spatial discrimination to one of controlled temporal sequencing, using engineered, erasable enzymatic labels on a unified nanosensor platform.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel sensing architecture based on Lieber's existing ultraflexible nanowire field-effect transistor (FET) probes. Each probe will be functionalized with a pool of aptamer-based capture strands for distinct neurochemical targets (e.g., dopamine, glutamate, serotonin). For each measurement cycle, a specific, enzyme-linked reporter (e.g., horseradish peroxidase) will be introduced via a microfluidic channel integrated into the probe shank. Binding produces a localized, amplified catalytic product (e.g., H2O2) detected amperometrically by the underlying nanowire. A subsequent wash with a chemical erasure agent (e.g., a reducing buffer) will cleave a designed linker, removing the enzyme and resetting the sensor surface for the next cycle, enabling programmable, time-division multiplexing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for high-multiplexity molecular sensing in vivo using radically simplified hardware, directly advancing the field of chronic brain-machine interfaces. It will produce a prototype injectable probe capable of sequentially quantifying at least four distinct neurochemicals with a single nanowire electrode, demonstrating a path towards dense chemical phenotyping with minimal implant footprint.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the dominant sensor design principle by intentionally using complex biochemical labels to achieve hardware simplicity, adapting a histopathology technique (cyclic immunofluorescence) for dynamic, in vivo electronic sensing—a concept not explored in neural interface engineering pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Reconfigurable, In Vivo Biosensor Mesh for Dynamic Multi-Analyte Neurochemical Recording
proposals.motivation: Current neural probes for in vivo chemical sensing are limited by static, pre-defined receptor panels, analogous to Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs). This fixed architecture prevents adaptation to new biomarkers or sequential monitoring of different neurochemical pathways without explanting the device. A platform capable of post-implantation reconfiguration would revolutionize longitudinal studies of brain chemistry and dynamic disease states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a flexible, mesh-like electronic array, integrated with microfluidic channels, can be dynamically and selectively functionalized with different bioreceptors in situ, enabling a single implanted device to be sequentially programmed for distinct multi-analyte sensing tasks.
proposals.method: We will fabricate an ultraflexible, injectable mesh sensor using Lieber's established nanowire FET technology, creating a foundational grid of unspecialized, addressable electrodes. Integrated microfluidic channels will deliver distinct bioreceptor solutions (e.g., antibodies, aptamers) to specific electrode nodes, where they will be anchored via electrochemically activated coupling chemistry. A closed-loop control system, inspired by FPGA place-and-route algorithms, will manage the fluidic routing and functionalization sequence based on user-defined assay panels.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first demonstration of a fully reconfigurable, in vivo biosensing platform, moving beyond fixed-function devices. It will establish a new paradigm for adaptive neurochemical interfaces, enabling long-term implants to evolve with experimental needs and providing unprecedented flexibility for systems neuroscience and neuromodulation studies.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is translating the FPGA's post-fabrication programmability concept to a biological sensor, merging dynamic microfluidic functionalization with chronic neural interface technology to create a device whose specificity is not fixed at manufacture.

proposals.title: Label-Free, Optically Resonant Nanowire Mesh for Multiplexed Neurochemical Sensing in Brain Tissue
proposals.motivation: Existing nanowire-based neural probes rely on direct electrical readout, which can be limited by signal-to-noise, multiplexing density, and long-term stability in the complex ionic brain environment. A purely optical readout strategy, analogous to established Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) biosensing, could circumvent these electrical challenges. This would enable high-fidelity, chronic monitoring of neurotransmitters and neuromodulators with minimal tissue perturbation, a critical unmet need in neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a flexible, mesh-like array of semiconductor nanowires, functionalized for specific neurochemical capture, can act as a network of optical whispering-gallery-mode resonators. The binding of target analytes will induce a quantifiable shift in each nanowire's optical resonance frequency, detectable via integrated photonics, providing a sensitive and multiplexed readout mechanism.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible meshes of silicon nanowires using established vapor-liquid-solid growth, integrating them onto biocompatible polymer substrates. Nanowires will be chemically functionalized with specific capture agents (e.g., aptamers for dopamine). Optical resonance will be characterized using a tunable laser coupled to the mesh via evanescent field from an integrated silicon nitride waveguide. A custom algorithm will perform real-time Lorentzian fitting to scattering spectra from individual nanowires to track resonance wavelength shifts with picometer resolution, enabling multiplexed detection across the array.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for biointerfacing that decouples the sensing element from the electrical readout, potentially improving biocompatibility and signal stability. It will deliver a proof-of-concept, multiplexed optical sensor platform specifically designed for integration into neural tissue, expanding the toolbox for high-resolution neurochemical mapping.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in transposing the core principle of label-free SPR detection into a flexible, tissue-integrable nanowire mesh format, moving beyond rigid SPR chips and purely electrical nanowire sensors. It uniquely combines Lieber's expertise in brain-compatible nanowire devices with photonic biosensing concepts not previously applied in this context.

proposals.title: Nanowire Field-Effect Transistor Arrays for Multiplexed, Non-Electrical Detection of Neurochemical and Proteomic Signatures
proposals.motivation: Direct electrical recording of neural activity, while foundational, provides an incomplete picture of brain function, missing critical neurochemical and molecular signaling. Existing methods for multiplexed molecular sensing in vivo, such as microdialysis coupled to mass spectrometry, lack the spatial and temporal resolution of modern electrophysiology. A platform that merges the high-density, minimally invasive integration of Lieber's nanowire electronics with a non-electrical readout could unlock simultaneous monitoring of electrical and chemical activity.
proposals.hypothesis: Functionalized, ultraflexible nanowire field-effect transistor (NW-FET) arrays can serve as a high-fidelity capture platform for specific biomolecules, and that their binding-induced conductance changes can be transduced and quantified via integrated photonic or magnetic resonance-based readout modalities, bypassing the need for direct electrical wiring.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh-like, injectable NW-FET arrays based on established silicon nanowire synthesis. Specific NW surfaces will be functionalized with aptamers or antibodies for targets like dopamine, glutamate, or neurotrophic factors. Instead of direct electrical connection, we will integrate each NW-FET with a nanoscale radiofrequency (RF) resonator or a fluorescent nanodiamond (FND) containing nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers. Biomolecule binding will shift the FET's local charge environment, altering the resonator's frequency or the FND's spin resonance, which will be detected externally via MRI or wide-field fluorescence microscopy, respectively.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of biointerface that decouples signal capture from electrical readout, enabling truly multiplexed, long-term molecular sensing in deep tissue without progressive tethering failure. It will provide a foundational methodology for correlating neurochemical dynamics with high-bandwidth electrical recordings from the same nanoscale platform, advancing systems neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While NW-FETs are established as electrochemical sensors, their use as passive, wirelessly interrogated capture elements within an injectable mesh architecture is unexplored. The novelty lies in the hybrid integration of nanoscale electronic capture with optical/magnetic transduction, moving beyond the paradigm of direct electrical connection for every sensor node.

proposals.title: A Closed-System, Microfluidic Cartridge for Robust Point-of-Care Diagnostics Using Functionalized Nanowire Field-Effect Transistors
proposals.motivation: Semiconductor nanowire field-effect transistors (NW-FETs) offer exquisite sensitivity for label-free biomolecular detection but have struggled to transition from controlled lab environments to real-world point-of-care (POC) use. The primary failure modes are sensor fouling and signal degradation from complex, raw biological matrices (e.g., blood, saliva), which require skilled manual sample preparation and precise fluidic handling that are impractical for field deployment by non-experts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating NW-FET sensor arrays into a self-contained, disposable microfluidic cartridge that automates all sample preparation and fluidic control will overcome matrix interference, enabling laboratory-grade assay performance and reliability in non-laboratory settings.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a credit-card-sized, injection-molded polymer cartridge containing microfluidic channels, a membrane-based filtration unit, on-board reservoirs for calibration and regeneration buffers, and a sealed chamber housing a silicon nanowire FET array functionalized for a model protein target (e.g., cardiac troponin). A reusable, handheld reader will interface with the cartridge via pneumatic actuators and electrical contacts, executing a pre-programmed fluidic protocol: raw sample intake, filtration/dilution, controlled delivery to the sensor surface, buffer washing, and in-situ sensor recalibration. System performance will be validated using spiked human whole blood and saliva samples, benchmarking sensitivity and specificity against gold-standard ELISA.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a fully integrated, closed-system prototype that demonstrates the practical POC viability of nanowire biosensors. It will provide a generalizable microfluidic and electronic architecture to decouple sophisticated sensor operation from user expertise, directly addressing the critical barrier of matrix effects. This represents a significant step toward deployable, quantitative diagnostic tools for resource-limited settings.
proposals.novelty: While microfluidics for sample prep and standalone NW-FET sensors exist, their integration into a single, self-calibrating, disposable cartridge that manages the entire assay workflow—from raw sample to electronic readout—for a fragile, label-free sensor like a NW-FET is novel and non-trivial, moving beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations to engineered system reliability.

proposals.title: A Portable, Closed-Loop Point-of-Care System for Real-Time Neurochemical Monitoring Using Injectable Nanowire Sensor Arrays
proposals.motivation: Current clinical monitoring of neurochemical dynamics, such as dopamine or glutamate signaling, is confined to bulky, stationary equipment in specialized labs, limiting its use in critical care settings, surgical suites, or field medicine. There is a pressing need for compact, portable diagnostic platforms that can provide real-time, high-fidelity chemical data at the bedside to guide treatment for neurological injury, stroke, or psychiatric episodes. Leveraging the Lieber group's proven ultraflexible and injectable nanowire-based probes, which offer minimal tissue damage and stable chronic recording, provides a unique opportunity to bridge this technological gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating ultraflexible, multiplexed nanowire field-effect transistor (NW-FET) sensor arrays with a miniaturized, wireless potentiostat and embedded signal processing unit will enable the creation of a portable, closed-loop point-of-care device capable of stable, real-time monitoring of multiple neurochemicals in ambulatory or clinical settings.
proposals.method: The project will integrate three core subsystems: 1) The sensing element, using existing Lieber-group methods to fabricate dense, multiplexed Si nanowire FET arrays functionalized with specific chemical receptors (e.g., for dopamine, glutamate) on ultraflexible polymer substrates for minimally invasive injection. 2) A custom-designed, miniaturized (smartphone-sized) electronic module housing a low-noise, multi-channel potentiostat for sensor biasing and data acquisition, a microcontroller for onboard signal processing (using real-time filtering and baseline correction algorithms), and Bluetooth Low Energy for wireless data transmission. 3) A tablet-based graphical user interface for real-time visualization and threshold-based alert generation, forming a closed-loop diagnostic platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first fully integrated, portable point-of-care device for multiplexed neurochemical sensing, moving laboratory-grade chemical electrophysiology into practical clinical and field environments. It will establish a new paradigm for closed-loop, biomarker-driven monitoring in neurology and critical care, directly impacting patient management strategies. The system will serve as a versatile platform for future integration with other nanowire-based physiological sensors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has demonstrated the biocompatibility and recording capability of injectable nanowire probes, their operation has remained tethered to large laboratory instrumentation. The novelty lies in the complete system-level integration of these advanced nanoscale sensors into a standalone, portable, and user-friendly diagnostic device, enabling practical use outside a traditional research lab.

proposals.title: Joint Sensor Physics and Source Deconvolution via Deep Inverse Modeling for Robust, Calibration-Light Neurochemical Sensing
proposals.motivation: Nanowire field-effect transistor (NWFET) arrays offer a powerful platform for multiplexed, real-time neurochemical recording in the brain. However, their deployment is hindered by sensor-to-sensor variability, cross-reactivity to complex biomarker mixtures, and the need for extensive, unstable calibration. A new computational framework is needed to extract accurate signals from imperfect, low-cost hardware in dynamic biological environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep inverse model, trained to jointly estimate the unique binding profile of each nanowire sensor and the underlying biomarker concentrations from raw, overlapping signals, will transform non-uniformity and cross-reactivity into informative features, enabling robust deconvolution without exhaustive per-device calibration.
proposals.method: We will develop a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture where the encoder maps high-dimensional, time-series sensor array data to a latent space representing both the unknown biomarker source concentrations and a set of sensor-specific physical parameters (e.g., sensitivity vectors, cross-talk coefficients). The decoder will implement a physics-informed forward model of the sensor array's response. Training will use synthetically generated data from known sensor physics models and experimentally collected NWFET array responses to controlled mixtures of neurotransmitters (e.g., dopamine, glutamate). Optimization will employ a combined loss function incorporating reconstruction error and physical plausibility constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified software-hardware framework that significantly reduces the calibration burden for multiplexed neurochemical sensing. It will advance the field of neural interfaces by enabling more reliable interpretation of complex, in vivo chemical signals from next-generation flexible probes. The core algorithmic approach will be generalizable to other biosensor platforms facing similar inverse problems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior sequential calibration methods, this approach formulates the entire measurement system as a single, end-to-end learnable inverse problem. It explicitly leverages, rather than merely correcting for, sensor imperfections as a source of diversity for signal separation, a concept not fully explored in pre-2026 neurotechnology.

proposals.title: DNA-Origami-Templated Transfer Fabrication of Ultra-Dense, Addressable Nanowire Arrays for Neural Interfacing
proposals.motivation: Current brain-machine interfaces face a fundamental trade-off between high-density, multiplexed recording and minimal tissue damage. While CMOS electronics offer multiplexing, their rigidity and bulk conflict with the need for flexible, minimally invasive probes. Existing transfer fabrication methods lack the precision to integrate ultra-dense, individually addressable nanowire sensors from a CMOS wafer onto a flexible, bio-compatible substrate without compromising interconnect density or alignment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sacrificial, programmable DNA origami scaffold, patterned on a donor CMOS wafer, can act as a precision template to define the geometry and interconnects for an ultra-dense nanowire array, enabling its intact transfer and integration onto flexible polymer substrates for neural recording.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize DNA origami structures to form a patterned, adhesive scaffold on a silicon CMOS wafer containing prefabricated electrode pads. Metallic or semiconductor nanowires will be precisely positioned onto the scaffold via sequence-specific binding or electrophoretic assembly. A flexible parylene-C film will be deposited over the array, and the entire stack will be released via electrochemical dissolution of a sacrificial layer beneath the CMOS electronics. Finally, the DNA scaffold will be enzymatically degraded, leaving the ultra-dense, addressable nanowire array embedded in the flexible film, ready for implantation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for merging high-performance silicon electronics with flexible bio-interfaces, directly enabling the creation of next-generation neural probes with unprecedented spatial resolution and channel count. It will provide a generalizable nanofabrication platform for integrating diverse nanostructures with CMOS backplanes on arbitrary, soft substrates.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the adaptation of programmable DNA origami—a tool from structural nanotechnology—as a sacrificial, high-resolution template within a CMOS transfer process. This hybrid approach is distinct from prior polymer-based transfer methods and enables nanometer-precision alignment and interconnect routing that is currently unattainable.

proposals.title: Wafer-Scale, Ultraflexible Nanowire Transistor Arrays for High-Throughput, Single-Cell Electrophysiological Screening
proposals.motivation: Current high-density neural probes, while advanced, are limited to thousands of recording sites, insufficient for screening large libraries of drugs or genetic constructs across massive cellular populations. Pre-2026, there is a critical need for tools that can perform functional phenotyping at the scale of millions of individual cells to accelerate neuroscience and drug discovery. Scaling up the proven flexible, mesh-like electronics pioneered by Lieber's group could bridge this gap between high-fidelity single-cell recording and industrial-scale throughput.
proposals.hypothesis: That the principles of stitching and injecting flexible, mesh electronics can be adapted to create planar, wafer-scale arrays of millions of individually addressable, ultraflexible nanowire field-effect transistors (NW-FETs), enabling stable, long-term, and massively parallel extracellular recording from cultured neuronal networks or dissociated cells.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible mesh electronics on 8-inch silicon wafers using pre-2026 semiconductor processing, integrating millions of silicon NW-FETs defined by top-down lithography. A key innovation will be developing a novel 'release-and-transfer' technique to detach the entire mesh from the wafer and integrate it with a custom CMOS multiplexer chip featuring switch-matrix architectures for scalable addressing. The system will be validated by recording from primary rodent neurons cultured directly on the transferred mesh, assessing signal-to-noise ratio, cell viability, and long-term stability over weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first demonstration of a million-channel, flexible bioelectronic array, establishing a new platform for high-content screening of neuronal activity in response to vast libraries of neuroactive compounds or optogenetic stimuli. It will extend the paradigm of injectable mesh electronics to in vitro applications, providing an unprecedented tool for systems neuroscience and pharmaceutical research. The developed fabrication and integration methods will serve as a blueprint for scaling up flexible bioelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While others have made dense CMOS arrays, the integration of Lieber-group's signature ultraflexible, biocompatible nanowire mesh at wafer-scale for screening is unprecedented. The approach uniquely combines the mechanical and cellular integration advantages of mesh electronics with the massive parallelism of industrial semiconductor manufacturing, a non-obvious synthesis of two previously distinct technological tracks.

proposals.title: Tomographic Calibration of Nanowire Neurochemical Sensor Arrays via Multi-Biomarker Projections
proposals.motivation: Nanowire field-effect transistor (NW-FET) arrays offer unprecedented sensitivity for multiplexed neurochemical recording in vivo. However, their quantitative accuracy is fundamentally limited by device-to-device and intra-array variability in surface chemistry and electronic response, which are difficult to characterize post-fabrication. A robust, scalable method to functionally map the heterogeneous binding landscape of a sensor surface is needed to translate these devices into reliable tools for systems neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial non-uniformity of a nanowire sensor array's sensitivity and binding affinity can be reconstructed as a functional tomographic image by applying a known panel of biomarkers in controlled sequences and analyzing the resulting multi-dimensional binding profiles using inverse problem algorithms.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible mesh electronics incorporating NW-FET arrays. A microfluidic system will deliver sequential 'projections' of distinct biomarkers (e.g., dopamine, glutamate, pH buffers) in known concentrations and spatiotemporal patterns across the array. The inverse problem—mapping the 2D sensitivity function from the set of 1D temporal binding profiles—will be solved using established tomographic reconstruction algorithms, specifically an adapted Algebraic Reconstruction Technique (ART) and Filtered Back Projection. The derived functional map will then be used to calibrate real-time neurochemical measurements in acute brain slice preparations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational calibration framework for quantitative, multiplexed biosensing with nanowire arrays, directly enhancing the fidelity of chronic brain-computer interface recordings. It will provide a new methodology to deconvolve sensor heterogeneity from biological signal, a critical step for the reliable deployment of these devices in complex biological environments like the brain.
proposals.novelty: While tomography is standard in medical imaging, its application to solve the inverse problem of sensor surface characterization, using biomarkers as projection sources, is a novel conceptual leap for nano-bioelectronics. This moves calibration beyond simple electrical testing to a functional, chemistry-specific mapping.

proposals.title: In Situ Kinetic Calibration of Nanowire Field-Effect Transistor Arrays for Quantitative Enzyme Profiling
proposals.motivation: Nanowire field-effect transistor (NW-FET) arrays offer exquisite sensitivity for label-free biomolecular detection, but their quantitative application is hampered by device-to-device heterogeneity in surface chemistry and electronic response. This variability, arising from fabrication inconsistencies, prevents reliable comparison of subtle kinetic parameters—like binding rates or affinities—across different sensors, limiting their use in precise diagnostic assays for enzyme activity or inhibitor screening. A method to dynamically map and correct this functional heterogeneity is needed to transform NW-FET arrays into quantitatively reliable platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: That the characteristic kinetic binding profile of a well-defined enzyme (e.g., a protease with a standardized substrate) can serve as a universal dynamic fingerprint to computationally map the functional response parameters of each sensor in a NW-FET array, enabling the correction of raw signals for quantitative comparison of unknown enzyme samples.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible, multiplexed silicon nanowire FET arrays using established methods from the Lieber group. The array will be functionalized for a target enzyme system (e.g., thrombin). A calibration step will involve perfusing the array with a solution containing the enzyme and its substrate at known concentrations, recording real-time conductance transients from each nanowire. An inverse problem-solving algorithm, based on a modified Michaelis-Menten framework that incorporates nanowire-specific scaling and offset parameters, will fit the multi-site kinetic data to generate a spatial map of sensitivity and effective binding constant for each sensor. Subsequent assays of unknown samples will apply this map to correct raw signals before extracting kinetic parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel calibration paradigm that moves beyond static electrical characterization to dynamic biochemical referencing, directly addressing the core challenge of sensor heterogeneity. It will deliver a robust computational framework for extracting quantitative kinetic data from heterogeneous NW-FET arrays, significantly advancing their utility in pharmaceutical inhibitor screening and the detection of pathological enzyme variants with subtle functional differences.
proposals.novelty: While post-hoc normalization of sensor signals is common, using the real-time kinetic signature of a biological reaction itself as a calibrant to solve for device-specific parameters is a novel bio-analytical strategy. This method intrinsically calibrates the functional bio-interface, not just the electronic baseline, which is non-obvious from existing electrical or single-point calibration techniques.

proposals.title: A Minimally Invasive, Cell-Based Neural Metabolic Sensor via Synthetic Biology and Flexible Optoelectronics
proposals.motivation: Current implantable neurochemical sensors face a fundamental scaling and biocompatibility challenge. Traditional microfabricated electrodes for metabolites like lactate or glucose are difficult to miniaturize for chronic, distributed recording without significant tissue damage and immune response. There is a critical need for new sensing paradigms that are inherently biocompatible and can be deployed at cellular scales within delicate neural tissue.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that encapsulating engineered, non-pathogenic bacterial cells containing synthetic genetic circuits for specific metabolite detection within a biocompatible hydrogel, and integrating this 'living sensor' with a flexible, implantable micro-optoelectronic readout device, will enable stable, long-term, and high-fidelity recording of local metabolic dynamics in the brain.
proposals.method: We will engineer E. coli Nissle 1917 with synthetic circuits expressing fluorescent proteins (e.g., GFP, RFP) under the control of metabolite-sensitive promoters (e.g., lldPRD for lactate). These cells will be encapsulated in alginate-poly-L-lysine microcapsules and embedded in a porous, soft PEG-based hydrogel matrix. This matrix will be integrated onto the tip of a miniaturized, ultraflexible polyimide neural probe, pre-2026 Lieber-group technology, incorporating microscale polymer waveguides and photodetectors. The probe will locally excite fluorescence and collect the emitted signal, with onboard circuitry for wireless data transmission.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of bio-hybrid neural interface that leverages biological machinery for sensing, offering superior biocompatibility and molecular specificity. It will provide a scalable platform for multiplexed, chronic monitoring of brain metabolism and signaling molecules, directly complementing existing electrophysiological recording tools. The project will demonstrate a novel integration pathway for synthetic biology and nanoelectronics in neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic biosensors and flexible electronics exist separately, their direct integration into a single, implantable device for *in vivo* neural recording is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the cell as the ultimate miniaturized sensor element and a custom flexible optoelectronic platform as the translator, creating a fundamentally new bioelectronic interface paradigm.

proposals.title: Injectable, Mesh-Integrated Nanowire Sensors for Real-Time, Multi-Analyte Monitoring in the Deep Brain
proposals.motivation: Continuous, real-time monitoring of neurochemicals (e.g., dopamine, glutamate) and metabolic markers (e.g., glucose, lactate) in deep brain structures is critical for understanding neurological disorders and developing closed-loop therapeutic devices. Existing implantable sensors often suffer from limited spatial resolution, chronic inflammatory responses, and an inability to monitor multiple analytes simultaneously from the same minimally invasive platform. A sensor that can be delivered via syringe to deep tissue and provide stable, long-term multiplexed recordings would address a major technological gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a freestanding, porous nanoelectronic mesh, functionalized with multiplexed sensing modalities on individual nanowire elements, can be injected into brain tissue to form a stable, biocompatible interface for the simultaneous, real-time monitoring of multiple biomarkers with minimal chronic immunoreactivity.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible, porous mesh sensors using Lieber-group established methods for silicon nanowire field-effect transistor (SiNW FET) arrays. Individual nanowires will be differentially functionalized with specific receptor chemistries (e.g., enzymes for metabolites, synthetic antibodies for cytokines). The mesh will be designed to be syringe-injectable through sub-100-micron cannulae, leveraging prior work on neural mesh electronics. In vivo validation will involve stereotaxic injection into the rodent striatum, with concurrent electrical recording of neurotransmitter dynamics (via fast-scan cyclic voltammetry on dedicated NWs) and continuous monitoring of lactate/pH. Chronic stability and glial encapsulation will be assessed histologically over 8+ weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of minimally invasive, multiplexed biosensor for deep-tissue implantation, directly translating the scalability of nanoelectronics into the domain of continuous biochemical sensing. It will provide a foundational platform for studying correlated electrophysiological and neurochemical dynamics in behaving animals and pave the way for closed-loop bioelectronic therapies that respond to chemical, not just electrical, signals.
proposals.novelty: While Lieber's mesh electronics have revolutionized chronic electrical recording, their integration with multiplexed, real-time *biochemical* sensing on the same injectable platform is unexplored. The novelty lies in merging the established biocompatible mesh architecture with a library of nanowire-based biosensors to create a unified, multifunctional 'neurochemical observatory'.

proposals.title: Fluidic-Directed and Molecularly Templated Assembly of Nanowire Neural Probes for Minimally Invasive Brain Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The development of ultraflexible, injectable neural probes by the Lieber group has enabled unprecedented chronic brain recording and stimulation. However, the assembly and integration of nanowire-based devices often rely on external electric fields, which can be incompatible with sensitive biological environments, limit spatial resolution, and constrain the geometries of assembled structures. A field-free assembly method is critical for creating next-generation bio-interfaces directly within complex, delicate tissue matrices without inducing electrochemical side effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that programmable microfluidic shear forces and sequence-specific DNA molecular recognition can direct the precise, hierarchical assembly of functionalized semiconductor nanowires into predefined 2D and 3D network architectures, eliminating the need for external electric fields.
proposals.method: We will design a microfluidic platform with tunable flow profiles to align nanowires via hydrodynamic focusing and shear-induced ordering. Complementary to this, nanowires will be functionalized with single-stranded DNA 'barcodes' at specific locations along their axis. Introducing linker strands with complementary sequences will drive spontaneous, selective binding to form interconnected networks on biodegradable polymer scaffolds. The assembly process will be characterized in situ using high-speed microscopy, and the resulting structures will be integrated with existing flexible electronics platforms for electrical validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for bio-compatible, bottom-up nanofabrication of neural devices. It will provide a toolkit for creating complex, 3D nanowire networks that can be seamlessly 'stitched' into neural tissue, advancing the design of injectable probes for distributed recording and stimulation. The method will decouple device assembly from electrophysiological operation, enhancing long-term stability and biocompatibility.
proposals.novelty: While fluidic assembly and DNA origami are known, their combined application for creating electrically functional, implantable nanowire neural probes without any electric field guidance is novel. This approach moves beyond passive nanostructure formation to actively program the connectivity and topology of an active recording device.

proposals.title: Nanoscale Heterojunction Engineering for Selective Ohmic Contacts to n- and p-Type Silicon Nanowires in Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: The Lieber group's pioneering work on ultraflexible, injectable, and stitchable nanowire-based neural probes requires reliable, high-density electrical interfaces. Current contact schemes often lack selectivity, leading to inconsistent signal transduction when interfacing with complex neural tissue containing both excitatory and inhibitory signals. Pre-2026, the precise control of metal-semiconductor junction properties at the nanoscale remains a critical bottleneck for scaling and improving the signal-to-noise ratio of multiplexed, multifunctional neural recording devices.
proposals.hypothesis: By engineering the metal work function and interfacial chemistry, we can create distinct, stable contact metallurgies that form low-resistance ohmic contacts to n-type SiNWs and specific, reproducible Schottky barrier heights to p-type SiNWs within the same device architecture.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible mesh electronics featuring parallel arrays of intrinsic, n-doped, and p-doped silicon nanowires (SiNWs). Using electron-beam lithography and thermal evaporation, we will deposit and pattern discrete contact pads of low-work-function metals (e.g., Ti/Al) and high-work-function metals (e.g., Pt, Pd) onto designated nanowire segments. The interfacial chemistry will be controlled via in-situ plasma treatments and rapid thermal annealing. Electrical characterization (I-V curves) will be performed to quantify contact resistance and barrier height, correlated with structural analysis via TEM and EDX.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational design rule for creating spatially selective, low-impedance contacts in multiplexed nanowire neural probes. It will directly enable the next generation of probes capable of simultaneous, artifact-free recording from distinct neuronal populations (e.g., glutamatergic vs. GABAergic), enhancing the fidelity of brain-machine interfaces. The developed contact schemes will be critical for implementing on-probe, nanowire-based transistor amplification.
proposals.novelty: While selective doping of nanowires is established, the deterministic engineering of nanoscale heterojunctions with tailored electronic properties *on a single, flexible neural probe substrate* is unexplored. This approach moves beyond generic metal deposition to create a functional contact taxonomy integrated directly into a biointerface device.

proposals.title: Scanning Probe Lithography for Deterministic Nanowire Contact Engineering in Ultraflexible Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: The Lieber group's pioneering ultraflexible nanowire-based neural probes require high-yield, precise electrical interfacing at the nanoscale.
