Conventional photolithography struggles with the non-planar, delicate nature of these nanostructures, often leading to unreliable contacts that limit device scalability and performance. A method for direct, maskless, and gentle patterning of metal contacts is needed to advance the manufacturability and reliability of these critical brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using an atomic force microscope (AFM) in a scanning probe lithography (SPL) mode can directly pattern conductive metal (e.g., Pt, Au) contacts onto individual semiconductor nanowires with sub-100 nm precision, resulting in contacts with lower impedance and higher mechanical stability than those fabricated by standard lift-off processes.
proposals.method: We will integrate a thermal evaporation source into a commercial AFM system equipped for Dip-Pen Nanolithography. Conductive polymer or metal-organic ink will be loaded onto the AFM tip and directly written onto predefined silicon nanowire devices on flexible SU-8 substrates. A subsequent oxygen plasma etch and mild thermal annealing will convert the patterned features into pure metal contacts. The process will be optimized by varying writing speed, force, and ink composition, with contact quality assessed via in-situ electrical characterization using the AFM tip as a movable electrode.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct-write, post-fabrication method for creating robust electrical contacts on fragile nanostructures, directly addressing a key bottleneck in scalable neural probe fabrication. It will provide quantitative data on the impedance, stability, and yield of SPL-fabricated nanowire contacts, enabling more complex, high-density neural interfaces. The technique could be extended to modify or repair contacts on already-implanted devices.
proposals.novelty: While SPL is known for patterning molecules, its direct application to solve the critical contact problem in state-of-the-art, implantable nanowire neural electronics is novel. This approach merges top-down nanofabrication with the bottom-up assembly paradigm central to the Lieber group's work, offering a unique path to device integration.

proposals.title: InP Nanowire Mesh Sensors for Real-Time, Multiplexed Neurochemical Recording in Freely Behaving Animals
proposals.motivation: Current neurochemical sensing technologies, such as carbon-fiber microelectrodes, are often rigid, single-point probes that cause chronic tissue damage and inflammation, limiting long-term, stable recording. There is a critical need for minimally invasive, multiplexed interfaces capable of monitoring dynamic neurotransmitter release (e.g., dopamine, serotonin) across large brain regions with high spatiotemporal resolution. Leveraging the Lieber group's expertise in ultraflexible, tissue-like mesh electronics, this proposal addresses this gap by repurposing the core nanomaterial for a new sensing modality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that surface-functionalized indium phosphide (InP) nanowires, when configured into a freestanding mesh electronics platform, can act as highly sensitive, selective, and stable field-effect transistor (FET)-based sensors for real-time neurochemical detection in vivo, due to their exceptional surface-area-to-volume ratio and tunable electronic properties.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible mesh electronics featuring InP nanowires as the active channel of FET sensors. Nanowire surfaces will be functionalized with specific capture agents (e.g., DNA aptamers or engineered enzymes) for target neurochemicals. Binding events will modulate nanowire conductance, transduced via integrated multiplexing circuitry. Devices will be injected into target brain regions (e.g., striatum, prefrontal cortex) of rodents, and sensor performance (sensitivity, selectivity, drift) will be benchmarked against established fast-scan cyclic voltammetry during calibrated behavioral tasks and pharmacological challenges.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new class of chronic, biocompatible neurochemical probes that merge the spatial coverage and tissue-integration advantages of mesh electronics with molecular sensing. It will provide a foundational platform for multiplexed monitoring of chemical signaling networks in the brain over timescales from seconds to months, enabling unprecedented studies of neuromodulation in behavior and disease models.
proposals.novelty: While the Lieber group has pioneered InP nanowire mesh electronics for electrical recording and stimulation, their application as functionalized, FET-based chemical sensors in a freestanding, injectable mesh format is unexplored. This represents a significant materials and device engineering pivot from transistor-based amplification to direct, label-free biochemical transduction.

proposals.title: Epitaxial Solid-State Scaffolding for Deterministic Synthesis of Hybrid Neural Nanowire Probes
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of complex, hybrid nanowires for neural interfaces is constrained by the dominant vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) paradigm, which limits material combinations and precise control over crystallographic orientation and heterojunction quality. This impedes the development of next-generation, multifunctional probes that require seamless integration of dissimilar electronic, ionic, and optoelectronic materials. A new synthesis strategy is needed to overcome these fundamental materials science limitations and enable advanced bioelectronic devices.
proposals.hypothesis: A pre-formed, single-crystalline nanowire scaffold can act as a solid-state epitaxial template to deterministically guide the growth of a secondary material via solid-state diffusion or confined solution-phase reactions, inverting the VLS paradigm and enabling novel material combinations and geometries for neural probes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate sacrificial silicon or germanium nanowire scaffolds via top-down lithography and etching for precise dimensional control. Using chemical vapor deposition and pulsed laser deposition, we will epitaxially grow target electronic materials (e.g., transition metal oxides, III-V semiconductors) directly onto these scaffolds via solid-state reactions. The process will be characterized in situ using environmental transmission electron microscopy to elucidate growth mechanisms. The resulting hybrid nanowires will be integrated into ultraflexible mesh electronics and their electrical and mechanical properties benchmarked against VLS-grown controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for 1D nanomaterial synthesis, moving beyond catalyst-mediated VLS growth. It will provide a materials science foundation for creating previously inaccessible heterostructured nanowires with atomically sharp interfaces, directly benefiting the design of high-fidelity, multifunctional neural recording and stimulation devices. The methodology could unlock new material properties by decoupling synthesis from the thermodynamic constraints of liquid catalyst alloys.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the fundamental nanowire growth mechanism from a catalyst-driven, bottom-up process to a top-down templated, solid-state epitaxial process, drawing a direct analogy from tissue engineering. It explicitly uses a pre-formed solid nanowire as the primary growth director, a concept distinct from post-synthesis assembly or coating of existing nanowires.

proposals.title: Closed-Loop Robotic Synthesis and Transport Mapping of Disordered Nanowires for Next-Generation Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The development of ultraflexible, minimally invasive neural probes, as pioneered by the Lieber group, requires electronic materials that combine high performance with mechanical compliance and biocompatibility. Traditional single-crystal nanowires, while excellent conductors, can be limited in their strain tolerance and synthesis complexity. Disordered nanowires (amorphous, polycrystalline) offer potential advantages in flexibility and scalable fabrication but are poorly understood, with their transport properties often dismissed as inferior. A systematic exploration of disorder as a tunable parameter could unlock new material platforms for advanced bioelectronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable metrics of structural disorder (e.g., medium-range order, grain boundary density, strain fields) can be engineered via synthesis to produce nanowires with superior or novel electronic/ionic transport properties—such as enhanced strain-insensitive conductance or selective ion permeability—that are optimal for chronic neural interfacing.
proposals.method: We will develop a high-throughput robotic platform for vapor-liquid-solid and solution-based synthesis of silicon and silicon-germanium nanowire libraries, varying composition, cooling rate, and substrate stress to create a continuum of disorder. An integrated characterization suite will perform automated four-point probe transport measurements and in-situ TEM/EELS analysis to extract disorder metrics (radial distribution functions, bond-angle variance). These data will train a Bayesian optimization model to navigate the synthesis-structure-property landscape, forming a closed-loop system that proposes new synthesis targets to maximize target transport figures of merit for neural recording.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first predictive map linking synthesis conditions to disorder metrics and functional transport in nanowires. It will deliver new design rules for disordered nanowire-based electronics, potentially identifying material regimes with unique properties for biointerfaces. The validated, disorder-engineered nanowires will provide a new material foundation for the next generation of ultraflexible, high-fidelity neural probes.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating disorder not as a defect to be minimized but as a multidimensional design variable to be actively optimized using a closed-loop, high-throughput experimental framework, directly challenging the single-crystal paradigm in nanoelectronics for biomedical applications.

proposals.title: Glass-Ceramic-Inspired Composite Nanowires for Mechanically Robust and Electrically Tunable Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: The development of ultraflexible, injectable neural probes from single-crystalline nanowires has enabled unprecedented chronic brain recording and stimulation. However, a fundamental materials limitation persists: the trade-off between the high electrical performance of perfect crystals and the mechanical compliance and defect tolerance of amorphous materials. This project addresses this by creating a new class of composite nanowire materials, inspired by the tailored properties of glass-ceramics, to transcend this trade-off for next-generation bio-interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deliberately engineering the nucleation, size, and distribution of crystalline domains within an amorphous semiconductor nanowire matrix, we can synthesize composite nanowires whose electronic transport and mechanical properties are superior to those of purely amorphous or single-crystalline phases, specifically optimized for chronic neural device applications.
proposals.method: We will adapt vapor-liquid-solid and templated electrodeposition synthesis of silicon and germanium nanowires, precisely modulating growth temperature and precursor flow to control amorphous-to-crystalline phase transitions. Post-growth thermal annealing under controlled atmospheres will be used to nucleate and grow nanocrystalline regions. The structure will be characterized via high-resolution TEM, Raman spectroscopy, and XRD. Electronic properties will be measured with in-situ nanoprobes, and mechanical compliance will be tested via AFM nanoindentation on freestanding nanowire meshes. Finally, we will fabricate and test prototype mesh electrodes for neural recording in rodent models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for designing nanowire-based neural interfaces by moving beyond the pursuit of perfect crystallinity. It will deliver a fundamental synthesis-structure-property framework for glass-ceramic-like nanowires and demonstrate a new material platform that synergistically combines high electrical performance with exceptional mechanical durability for chronic brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel because it directly challenges the prevailing nanowire design dogma of maximizing crystallinity, instead applying principles from macroscopic structural composites to nanoscale electronic materials. It intentionally creates and leverages disorder and interfaces within a single nanowire to achieve tailored functionality.

proposals.title: Amorphous and Polycrystalline Silicon Nanowire Mesh Electronics for Enhanced Durability in Chronic Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Lieber's pioneering work on ultraflexible, injectable mesh electronics for brain recording relies on single-crystalline semiconductor nanowires (NWs) for their excellent electronic properties. However, the synthesis of high-quality single crystals is complex and costly, potentially limiting scalability and mechanical robustness under chronic in vivo stress. Exploring non-crystalline NWs could unlock new material sets with superior fracture toughness and easier fabrication, critical for long-term bio-integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amorphous silicon (a-Si) and polycrystalline silicon (poly-Si) nanowires, fabricated via scalable thin-film processes and nanolithography, can form mesh electronics with electrical performance sufficient for neural recording and mechanical durability exceeding that of single-crystalline counterparts.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a-Si and poly-Si NWs using a top-down approach: depositing thin films on sacrificial layers, patterning NW meshes via electron-beam lithography, and releasing them as free-standing scaffolds. Key metrics—conductivity, transistor mobility, and impedance—will be benchmarked against single-crystal Si NW controls. Mechanical durability will be tested through cyclic bending and simulated cerebrospinal fluid immersion. Finally, we will functionalize meshes with Pt electrodes and validate them for acute local field potential recording in rodent cortex.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a material library for neural probes beyond single crystals, directly addressing chronic failure modes. It will provide a comparative framework for the trade-offs between electronic performance, mechanical resilience, and manufacturability in bio-integrated electronics, potentially lowering the barrier to clinical translation.
proposals.novelty: While the field has focused on perfect crystalline nanomaterials for optimal electronics, we systematically investigate the non-obvious utility of structurally disordered nanowires, prioritizing mechanical integration and scalable fabrication for neural interfaces without sacrificing core functionality.

proposals.title: Low-Temperature Synthesis and Photonic Post-Processing of Nanowire Mesh Electronics for Enhanced Chronic Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Integrating high-performance electronic materials with ultraflexible, bio-integrated neural probes is limited by the thermal sensitivity of polymer substrates and biological scaffolds. Conventional high-temperature processing for materials like crystalline silicon or metal oxides degrades these substrates, compromising device performance and biocompatibility. A method to decouple material quality from processing temperature is critical for next-generation chronic brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid synthesis route, combining low-temperature chemical vapor deposition of semiconductor nanowires with subsequent pulsed laser annealing (PLA), will yield nanowire networks with enhanced crystallinity and electronic mobility while preserving the integrity of ultraflexible polyimide or parylene substrates.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh electronics via guided assembly of silicon nanowires synthesized via a low-pressure, low-temperature (≤300°C) vapor-liquid-solid process on sacrificial layers. The assembled mesh will be transferred to a flexible substrate and interconnected with metal electrodes. A critical post-processing step will employ a scanned excimer laser (e.g., 248 nm KrF) for millisecond-scale pulsed annealing of the nanowires, selectively melting and recrystallizing the silicon to reduce defects and improve carrier mobility, while the substrate's brief, localized heating remains below its damage threshold. Electrical and mechanical characterization will benchmark the processed devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials processing framework for creating high-mobility semiconductor components on thermally sensitive platforms, directly enabling more powerful and energy-efficient active electronics for neural recording and stimulation. It will provide a generalizable pathway to integrate a wider range of functional materials into bio-interfacing devices, pushing the limits of scalability and chronic stability in brain-computer interfaces.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the decoupling strategy: using a non-thermal, spatially selective photonic energy source (pulsed laser annealing) to improve material properties *after* nanoscale integration onto a flexible substrate, a significant departure from prior work focused solely on low-temperature deposition or substrate-compatible but low-quality materials.

proposals.title: Gentle Synthesis of Antibody-Conjugated Nanowire Forests for In Vivo Multiplexed Electrochemical Detection of Labile Neuroinflammatory Biomarkers
proposals.motivation: Current neural probes, including Lieber's ultraflexible nanowire mesh electronics, excel at electrophysiological recording but lack the biochemical sensing capability needed to correlate neural activity with molecular events. Critically, key protein biomarkers for neuroinflammation and neurodegeneration (e.g., Aβ oligomers, tau, cytokines) are conformationally labile and degrade under the high-temperature or harsh chemical conditions used in standard nanomaterial synthesis and device fabrication. This creates a fundamental mismatch between advanced neural interfaces and the tools needed to detect the underlying molecular drivers of disease.
proposals.hypothesis: That novel, sub-40°C wet-chemical synthesis routes can be developed to grow conductive polymer-coated silicon nanowire forests directly on flexible neural probe substrates while preserving the full bioactivity of pre-conjugated antibody recognition elements, enabling the stable, multiplexed electrochemical detection of conformation-sensitive biomarkers in situ.
proposals.method: We will adapt Lieber's vapor-liquid-solid nanowire synthesis to a low-temperature, solution-based catalytic growth method on flexible SU-8/parylene substrates. Crucially, antibodies for target biomarkers (e.g., IL-1β, TNF-α) will be conjugated to nanowire tips prior to a subsequent, gentle electrochemical polymerization of poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) at room temperature, forming a conductive, protective matrix. This core 'bio-nanowire' element will be integrated into a multiplexed, flexible probe array. A custom potentiostat circuit will apply fast-scan cyclic voltammetry protocols to simultaneously resolve distinct redox signatures from captured biomarkers.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for 'biofunctional synthesis' that directly embeds sensitive biorecognition into the core of nanoscale neural interfaces. It will produce the first demonstration of a fully integrated, flexible probe capable of simultaneous electrical recording and multiplexed, real-time detection of labile protein biomarkers in the brain extracellular space, bridging a critical gap in systems neuroscience and neuropathology diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in reversing the standard sensor fabrication sequence—conjugating antibodies *before* the final conductive nanomaterial synthesis—and developing the specific low-temperature chemical pathways to make this possible, thereby preserving epitope recognition for targets destroyed by conventional processing.

proposals.title: Gradient-Property Nanowire Probes for Spatially Resolved Neural Recording and Stimulation
proposals.motivation: Current ultraflexible neural probes, including nanowire-based devices, typically employ uniform-diameter elements, limiting their ability to match the spatially varying mechanical and electrical properties of brain tissue. This mismatch can lead to chronic immune responses and suboptimal signal quality at different depths. A probe with intentionally modulated geometry could better integrate across tissue gradients, from the pial surface to deep cortical layers, improving long-term stability and recording fidelity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fabricating silicon nanowires with controlled, tapered or sinusoidally modulated diameters will create a gradient in mechanical flexibility and electrical impedance along their length, enabling depth-dependent optimization for neural interfacing within a single, minimally invasive shank.
proposals.method: We will synthesize tapered silicon nanowires via a modulated vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth process, precisely controlling precursor gas flow to create linear and non-linear diameter profiles. These nanowires will be integrated into ultraflexible mesh electronics using established stitching techniques. We will characterize the mechanical bending stiffness and electrochemical impedance as a function of position along each nanowire. Finally, we will validate these gradient-property probes in vivo in rodent models, comparing their chronic recording performance and tissue response against uniform-diameter controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for neural probes that actively engineer geometry to match biological gradients. It will provide the first experimental dataset linking programmed nanowire diameter modulation to in vivo device performance and biocompatibility. The resulting probes could enable single-shank devices that simultaneously achieve high-fidelity single-unit recording at depth and low-impedance local field potential recording near the surface.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has optimized nanowire probes for minimal feature size and flexibility, the intentional use of non-uniform diameter to create functional gradients along a single nanowire element is unexplored. This moves beyond a 'one-size-fits-all' approach to a biomimetic, spatially adaptive design.

proposals.title: In Situ Nanomechanical Characterization of Individual Nanowires for Next-Generation Injectable Neural Probes
proposals.motivation: The development of ultraflexible, injectable neural probes based on nanowire electronics is a major advance in brain-machine interfaces, enabling chronic recording with minimal tissue damage. However, the long-term mechanical reliability of these devices depends critically on the intrinsic tensile strength and elasticity of their constituent nanowires, which remain poorly characterized at the single-element level. Direct experimental measurement is essential to guide the rational design of probes that can withstand implantation stresses and cyclic mechanical loads within the dynamic brain environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the tensile strength and elastic modulus of individual semiconductor nanowires, as used in neural probes, can be directly measured using a microelectromechanical systems (MEMS)-based tensile testing platform, and that these properties will significantly exceed those of bulk materials and show size-dependent scaling.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a silicon-on-insulator MEMS device with a thermal actuator and a capacitive force sensor. Individual nanowires (e.g., silicon, silicon-germanium) will be transferred and precisely aligned across a gap using a micromanipulator under a scanning electron microscope (SEM). The nanowire ends will be anchored via electron-beam-induced deposition of platinum. The MEMS actuator will apply controlled displacement while the force sensor measures load, all within the SEM for simultaneous visualization. Stress-strain curves will be derived from displacement and force data, yielding tensile strength and Young's modulus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative dataset on the mechanical properties of key nanowire materials used in flexible bioelectronics. It will establish a foundational metrology for predicting device failure and optimizing the structural design of injectable probes. The results will inform materials selection and nanowire geometry to enhance the robustness of future high-density neural interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While atomic force microscopy has been used for bending tests, this proposal leverages a MEMS-based tensile stage for direct, quantitative axial loading of single nanowires, a method not yet applied to the specific semiconductor nanowires used in Lieber's neural probes. The in-situ SEM approach allows for correlating mechanical failure with structural features.

proposals.title: Macroscopic, Injectable Nanowire Textile Meshes for Chronic, Large-Area Neural and Cardiac Interfacing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work in flexible bioelectronics, including Lieber's ultraflexible neural probes, has enabled minimally invasive interfacing with high spatial resolution. However, these devices are typically limited to single probes or small, planar arrays, constraining their ability to map distributed neural circuits or interface with large, dynamic organs like the heart. A scalable method to create large-area, conformable, and injectable electronic fabrics would bridge the gap between microscopic device flexibility and macroscopic functional coverage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting solution-based assembly and textile manufacturing techniques, we can produce freestanding, centimeter-scale fabrics from ultraflexible silicon nanowire (SiNW) building blocks that retain the electronic and mechanical properties of individual nanowires, enabling stable, chronic recording and stimulation over large tissue areas.
proposals.method: We will scale up the synthesis of kinked and coaxial SiNWs to gram quantities. Using a modified electrospinning apparatus, we will co-process these nanowires with biodegradable polymer fibers (e.g., PLGA) to create non-woven, porous mats. We will integrate these mats with a mesh of pre-fabricated, stretchable metal interconnects using transfer printing. The resulting macroscopic textile will be characterized mechanically (tensile testing, bending radius) and electronically (impedance, transistor mobility). Finally, we will validate functionality by minimally invasive injection and deployment over the cortex of rodent models for neural recording and onto the epicardium for cardiac electrophysiology mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational manufacturing pathway for macroscopic nanowire-based electronics, moving beyond single devices to large-area, tissue-integrated systems. It will demonstrate a new class of injectable, fabric-like neural and cardiac interfaces capable of conforming to complex 3D organ surfaces, enabling unprecedented spatial mapping and modulation. This directly extends the Lieber group's core mission of stitching electronics into the brain and body.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has created flexible patches or meshes from conventional materials, the creation of macroscopic, functional textiles from the same sophisticated, subcellular-scale nanowire components used for single-neuron recording is unprecedented. The key novelty is maintaining the nanoscale electronic superiority and extreme flexibility across a bulk, handleable material suitable for surgical or injectable deployment.

proposals.title: Controlled Unzipping of Nanowires into Conformal 2D Sheets for High-Density, Minimally Invasive Neural Interfaces
proposals.motivation: Lieber's group has pioneered ultraflexible, injectable neural probes based on 1D nanowires and nanotubes, enabling recording from deep brain structures with minimal tissue damage. However, achieving stable, high-density, multi-site recording over large, conformal areas of the cortical surface or within complex tissue folds remains a challenge. Transforming 1D nanostructures into 2D sheets could provide a material platform that combines the nanoscale feature size of wires with the expansive, conformal coverage of a mesh, potentially revolutionizing chronic brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and silicon nanowires (SiNWs) can be controllably longitudinally 'unzipped' or laterally assembled into freestanding, electrically conductive 2D nanoribbon sheets, and that these sheets will exhibit superior mechanical compliance and electrochemical stability for neural interfacing compared to their 1D precursors.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-step chemical-vapor-etching process to longitudinally unzip carbon nanotubes into graphene nanoribbon meshes, inspired by pre-2026 oxidative unzipping methods but optimized for preserved conductivity and flexibility. For silicon nanowires, we will explore a templated assembly and sintering approach to fuse wires into porous 2D sheets. These materials will be integrated into Lieber's mesh electronics platform using established stitching and injection techniques. Their electrical properties, mechanical compliance, and stability will be characterized, followed by validation in acute and chronic rodent models for cortical surface and intracortical recording.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new materials synthesis pathway for creating 2D neural interfaces from 1D building blocks. It will provide a fundamental understanding of how dimensionality affects bio-integration and recording fidelity. The resulting devices could enable unprecedented spatial coverage and signal quality for mapping distributed neural circuits, directly advancing the field of minimally invasive neuroelectronics.
proposals.novelty: While 2D materials like graphene have been explored for neurotech, the direct synthesis of 2D sheets from the very 1D nanowires that have already proven successful in Lieber's injectable probes is a novel materials strategy. It uniquely bridges the gap between the high-performance, validated 1D platform and the expanded functionality of 2D architectures, which is non-obvious given the distinct synthesis and handling requirements of each form.

proposals.title: Ultraflexible, Polarization-Sensitive Nanowire Probes for Near-Field Optical Imaging of Biological Nanostructures
proposals.motivation: Near-field scanning optical microscopy (NSOM) enables super-resolution imaging beyond the diffraction limit, but conventional metal-coated tapered fiber probes are bulky, fragile, and lack intrinsic polarization sensitivity for mapping biomolecular orientation. This limits their application in studying dynamic, nanoscale structures in soft biological tissues, such as protein aggregates in neural disease or extracellular matrix fibers. A new class of nanoscale, flexible optical probes is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-crystalline semiconductor nanowires, owing to their sub-wavelength diameter, waveguiding properties, and anisotropic optical response, can function as robust, polarization-sensitive near-field probes, enabling high-resolution optical mapping of nanostructure orientation in hydrated biological samples.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible probes by integrating individual III-V semiconductor nanowires (e.g., GaAs) onto the tapered tips of SU-8 or polymer-based microfilaments, leveraging Lieber group's expertise in nanowire synthesis and flexible bioelectronics stitching. The nanowire's crystalline axis will define a built-in polarization axis. We will develop a scanning system coupling 532 nm or 633 nm laser light into the nanowire's base and collect the modulated near-field signal via a sensitive avalanche photodiode. Scanning algorithms will correlate topographic data from the flexible filament's deflection with polarization-dependent optical signals to reconstruct vector optical fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for nanoscale bio-optical imaging, delivering the first fully flexible, polarization-resolving NSOM probe. It will enable direct, label-free correlation between nanoscale structure and optical anisotropy in native biological environments, advancing the study of amyloid fibril formation, collagen alignment in neural interfaces, and other structurally ordered biomaterials.
proposals.novelty: While nanowires have been used as passive waveguides or in rigid NSOM, their integration as the active, polarization-sensitive element on a flexible, tissue-compatible platform—directly translating Lieber's neural probe technology to optical microscopy—is a novel convergence of nano-optics and biointerface engineering.

proposals.title: Injectable, Plasmonically-Enhanced Nanowire Mesh for High-Bandwidth, Multiplexed Neurochemical Sensing
proposals.motivation: Existing ultraflexible electronic probes, like those pioneered by Lieber's group, excel at recording electrophysiology but offer limited multiplexed sensing of neurochemicals, which is critical for understanding brain circuitry and neuromodulation. Plasmonic nanostructures enable sensitive, label-free detection of molecular binding events via local refractive index changes, but are typically rigid and lack the chronic stability needed for in vivo brain interfaces. Combining these technologies could overcome these individual limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating semiconductor nanowires, which provide active transistor-based signal amplification and tissue-like flexibility, with metallic plasmonic nanostructures will create a hybrid device where plasmonic near-field enhancement drastically improves the signal-to-noise ratio for neurochemical detection, enabling high-spatiotemporal-resolution molecular sensing in vivo.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a hybrid mesh via deterministic nanomaterial assembly, embedding silicon nanowire field-effect transistors (FETs) within a porous, flexible polymer matrix. Gold nanorods or bowtie nanoantennas will be synthesized and precisely positioned at the nanowire FET channel surface via DNA-origami or electron-beam lithography patterning. The plasmonic structures will be functionalized with specific aptamers (e.g., for dopamine, glutamate). Device operation will involve illuminating the mesh with a tunable near-infrared laser diode; binding-induced plasmon shifts will modulate the local carrier density in the adjacent nanowire, transducing the optical signal into an amplified electrical readout via the FET.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield the first injectable, plasmonically-enhanced neural probe capable of simultaneous electrical recording and multiplexed, high-bandwidth neurochemical sensing. It will establish a new design paradigm for biointerfaces that leverages hybrid photonic-electronic transduction. The resulting devices will provide a critical tool for dissecting the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of neuromodulation in behaving animals.
proposals.novelty: While plasmonic sensors and flexible nanowire electronics exist separately, their monolithic integration into a single, injectable mesh for in vivo brain sensing is unprecedented. The novelty lies in using the nanowire not just as a passive scaffold, but as an active transducer that electrically reads out the enhanced optical near-field, creating a new class of polaritonic bio-sensor.

proposals.title: Wafer-Scale Fabrication of Monolithic, Stretchable Mesh Electronics for Large-Area Neural Interfacing
proposals.motivation: Current flexible and stretchable bioelectronic devices are fabricated on small, centimeter-scale substrates, limiting the creation of large-scale, seamless neural interfaces for mapping distributed brain circuits or covering expansive organ surfaces. Scaling to wafer-sized (e.g., 150-200 mm) monolithic systems would enable transformative applications in neuroscience and medicine, such as whole-cortex recording or conformal wrapping of peripheral nerves and organs, which are impossible with piecemeal assemblies. This addresses a critical manufacturing bottleneck in the field of chronic, biocompatible neural interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting and integrating pre-2026 processes—including sacrificial-layer patterning, deterministic nanowire assembly, and strain-isolating serpentine mesh design—into a unified, wafer-scale photolithographic workflow, we can fabricate single, functional, and stretchable electronic systems that maintain device yield and electronic performance across an entire silicon wafer.
proposals.method: The approach will adapt the 'mesh electronics' fabrication process, scaling it using large-area silicon handling wafers. Key steps include: 1) depositing and patterning a sacrificial polymer layer (e.g., polyimide) on a 200 mm wafer to define the mesh geometry; 2) using contact printing for deterministic, high-density transfer of pre-fabricated silicon nanowire transistors and platinum electrodes onto the sacrificial pattern; 3) depositing and patterning metal interconnects (Au/Cr) with pre-strained serpentine architectures; and 4) encapsulating the entire structure in a biocompatible elastomer (SU-8 or PDMS) before release via dissolution of the sacrificial layer, yielding a single, wafer-scale, free-standing mesh.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first scalable manufacturing platform for producing monolithic, stretchable electronic systems at the wafer scale, directly addressing a major materials integration challenge. It will provide a foundational tool for neuroscience, enabling future research into large-area, high-density brain-body interfaces. The developed protocols will be a significant advance for the broader field of biointegrated electronics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work from Lieber's group and others has created small, injectable mesh electronics, the monolithic integration of thousands of functional devices (nanowire transistors, electrodes) into a single, wafer-sized, stretchable system is unprecedented. The novelty lies in scaling a delicate, release-based process to a standard semiconductor manufacturing scale while preserving functionality and mechanical compliance.

proposals.title: Macroscopic, Injectable Bioelectronic Fibers from Aligned Nanowire Assemblies for Chronic Neural Interfacing
proposals.motivation: The Lieber group's recent work on ultraflexible, injectable nanowire probes has enabled minimally invasive access to deep brain structures and spinal cord. However, these devices are typically single, microscopic shanks, limiting the density of recording/stimulation sites and the total channel count for interfacing with large, distributed neural populations. Scaling the core nanoscale material advantages—flexibility, high surface area, and electronic performance—into macroscopic, multifunctional fibers would bridge a critical gap between nanoscale devices and practical, high-bandwidth brain-machine interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that macroscopic fibers, assembled from densely aligned and interconnected semiconductor nanowires or carbon nanotubes, will retain the favorable electrochemical and mechanical properties of individual nanoscale components, enabling the creation of injectable, chronic neural interfaces with orders-of-magnitude greater site density and functional complexity than current ultraflexible probes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate fibers using a modified thermal drawing process or iterative capillary-driven assembly, where silicon nanowires (pre-synthesized via vapor-liquid-solid growth) are aligned in a polymer matrix (e.g., polycarbonate or a biodegradable elastomer). Metallic nanowire interconnects will be integrated in parallel during assembly. The resulting fibers (50-200 µm diameter, several cm long) will be characterized mechanically and electronically. They will be functionalized with electrode sites and injected into rodent brain or spinal cord using adapted syringe techniques, followed by chronic electrophysiological recording and stimulation validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for scalable, minimally invasive bioelectronic interfaces, providing a material platform that combines nanoscale electronic performance with macroscopic, handleable form factors. It will directly extend the 'mesh electronics' and 'injectable probe' concepts to enable high-density, multiplexed interfacing with large neural networks, a key step toward advanced neuroprosthetics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work stitches or injects pre-fabricated nanoelectronic meshes, the bottom-up assembly of macroscopic, freestanding fibers from aligned nanoscale building blocks is unexplored. This approach decouples nanomaterial synthesis from device integration, potentially allowing for kilometer-scale production of neural interface 'thread.'

proposals.title: High-Throughput, Multiplexed Nanowire Field-Effect Transistor Arrays for Real-Time Screening of Neural Organoid Activity and Signaling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, brain organoids and 3D neural tissues offer unprecedented models for development and disease, but existing recording tools (e.g., planar MEAs, patch clamp) lack the spatial resolution, multiplexing capability, and long-term stability for high-throughput, functional phenotyping. There is a critical need for scalable, sensitive platforms to screen dynamic electrophysiological and neurochemical responses in complex 3D tissues under various pharmacological and genetic perturbations.
proposals.hypothesis: That scalable, ultraflexible nanowire field-effect transistor (NW-FET) arrays can be configured into multiplexed, multi-modal screening platforms capable of simultaneously recording extracellular action potentials and local neurotransmitter release (e.g., dopamine, glutamate) from hundreds of sites within 3D neural tissues with high sensitivity and temporal resolution.
proposals.method: We will fabricate large-scale (e.g., 256-1024 channel), ultraflexible mesh electronics based on Lieber-group silicon NW-FET technology, integrating distinct NW sensor elements functionalized with specific ion-selective membranes or enzymatic coatings for neurochemical detection. Arrays will be embedded within or interfaced with matrigel-supported neural organoids using 'stitching' or injection techniques. Custom acquisition hardware and software (leveraging pre-2026 spike-sorting algorithms like Kilosort and chemical signal deconvolution methods) will enable parallel, real-time readout. High-throughput screening will be demonstrated by applying libraries of neuromodulators and recording multiplexed electrophysiological and chemical responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for high-content functional screening of 3D neural systems, moving beyond simple spike counting to integrated electrophysiological and neurochemical phenotyping. It will provide a powerful tool for drug discovery, disease modeling (e.g., Parkinson's, epilepsy), and fundamental studies of neural network communication, directly building on Lieber's legacy of seamless tissue-device integration.
proposals.novelty: While NW-FETs are known for sensitivity, their integration into purpose-built, large-scale arrays for combined electrical/chemical screening in 3D tissues is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-design of the scalable mesh platform, multi-modal sensor functionalization, and high-throughput analytical framework specifically for organoid screening.

proposals.title: Multimodal Nanowire Probes for Simultaneous Conductance and Impedance Spectroscopy of Neurochemical Binding Kinetics
proposals.motivation: Current nanowire-based neural probes, pioneered by the Lieber group, excel at high-sensitivity, real-time recording of electrophysiology and neurotransmitter detection via field-effect conductance changes. However, conductance measurements alone provide limited information on the complex binding dynamics and interfacial changes at the probe-tissue or probe-analyte interface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful, label-free technique for characterizing surface interactions and biomolecular binding events, but it has not been fully integrated with the ultraflexible, minimally invasive nanowire platforms that are critical for stable chronic brain interfacing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating EIS measurement capabilities into ultraflexible nanowire field-effect transistors (FETs) will yield a multimodal readout that decouples binding kinetics from pure carrier concentration changes, providing a richer, more quantitative description of neurochemical interactions in vivo.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesh electronics incorporating silicon nanowire FETs with adjacent, passivated microelectrodes optimized for EIS. A custom, miniaturized multiplexing headstage and IC will be developed to interleave fast-sampling of nanowire conductance with frequency-swept impedance measurements (e.g., 1 Hz to 100 kHz) at the same recording site. Signal processing algorithms will employ equivalent circuit modeling (e.g., Randles circuit) of the impedance data and correlate time-domain features with concurrent FET transconductance changes to isolate binding-induced impedance shifts from bulk solution effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new methodology for multimodal sensing in flexible bioelectronics, directly yielding concurrent data streams on local neurotransmitter concentration (via FET) and binding state/affinity (via EIS). It will advance the quantitative analysis of neurochemical signaling in complex extracellular environments and provide a critical tool for validating the stability and performance of chronic neural interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While EIS and nanowire FETs exist separately, their co-integration into a single, ultraflexible, implantable probe for simultaneous, colocated measurement in vivo is novel. This approach moves beyond single-modality sensing to create a more complete picture of the bioelectronic interface.

proposals.title: A Multiplexed, Injectable Nanowire Electronic Tongue for Simultaneous Neurochemical Sensing in the Brain
proposals.motivation: Understanding brain function and pathology requires monitoring the complex interplay of multiple neurochemicals (e.g., dopamine, glutamate, serotonin) in real-time. Pre-2026 technologies, including single-analyte microelectrodes and fiber photometry, are limited in their multiplexing capability and often cause significant tissue damage due to their rigidity. There is a critical need for minimally invasive, high-density interfaces that can resolve the spatiotemporal dynamics of multiple signaling molecules concurrently.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a flexible, mesh-based array of differentially functionalized silicon nanowire field-effect transistors (SiNW FETs) can be injected into brain tissue to form a stable, multiplexed sensor capable of simultaneously and selectively recording the concentration dynamics of at least three distinct neurochemical analytes in vivo.
proposals.method: We will fabricate ultraflexible mesh electronics integrating dense arrays of SiNW FETs using established lithographic and release techniques from the Lieber lab. Distinct nanowires within the same mesh will be functionalized with different chemistries: e.g., platinum nanoparticles for H2O2 (dopamine proxy), glutamate oxidase, and ion-selective membranes for potassium. A custom multiplexing readout architecture, based on time-division or frequency-based addressing, will be developed to deconvolve signals from the co-located sensors. The device will be delivered via syringe injection into the rodent striatum, and its performance will be validated using pharmacological challenges (e.g., amphetamine, KCl) combined with established techniques like microdialysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first injectable, multiplexed electronic 'tongue' for the brain, enabling new experiments into cross-talk between neuromodulatory systems. It will advance the field of bioelectronic interfaces by moving beyond single-analyte detection and electrical recording alone, towards integrated chemical sensing. The platform will establish a foundation for closed-loop neuromodulation systems driven by chemical, rather than just electrical, neural activity.
proposals.novelty: While multiplexed SiNW sensor arrays exist for in vitro applications, their integration into an injectable, chronically stable mesh electronics platform for simultaneous multi-analyte sensing in the living brain is unprecedented. This merges the Lieber group's pioneering work in minimally invasive neural mesh electronics with the concept of a highly multiplexed electronic tongue, creating a new paradigm for chemical neural interfacing.

ideas.title: Bio-Integrated Microsensor Using Synthetic Biology Circuit Miniaturization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the miniaturization and integration principles of synthetic biology genetic circuits to solve the dual scaling challenge of an implantable sensor. In synthetic biology, researchers have engineered single cells to function as self-contained, ultra-miniaturized biosensors by incorporating genetic circuits that detect input molecules and produce optical outputs. This solution is adapted by designing a biocompatible, implantable sensor where the sensing element consists of encapsulated, engineered non-pathogenic cells containing circuits programmed to respond to specific biomarkers (e.g., glucose, lactate, inflammatory cytokines). The extreme scale-down is achieved by leveraging the cellular chassis itself. An implanted micro-optoelectronic device then reads the collective fluorescent or luminescent signal from the cell population in real-time, transmitting data externally. This approach bypasses traditional microfabrication limits for chemical sensing and utilizes biological machinery for detection and inherent biocompatibility.

ideas.title: Label-Free Signal Transduction via Target-Programmed Nanomechanical Gating
ideas.description: This approach eliminates the need for external labels by designing a sensor where specific biomolecular binding directly and reversibly actuates a nanoscale mechanical or electronic gate. The binding event itself causes a substantial, quantifiable change in a physical property like electrical conductivity or resonant frequency, engineered to produce a signal orders of magnitude larger than the binding energy. This retains the simplicity and directness of label-free detection while achieving the high signal-to-noise and simplified electronic readout typically associated with labels. The system thus inverts the need for labels by making the intrinsic detection mechanism inherently amplificatory.

ideas.title: Transfer CMOS fabrication for dense array integration using DNA origami scaffolding
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of transfer CMOS fabrication by drawing an analogy from the field of Structural Biology and Nanotechnology, specifically DNA origami assembly. The analogous solution uses programmable DNA folding to create precise scaffolds for positioning components. Adapted to the research context, the method involves designing and fabricating a sacrificial, patterned scaffold layer on a donor CMOS wafer. This scaffold acts as a temporary, precision template that defines the geometry and interconnects for an ultra-dense array of nanowires, which are integrated with the wafer's electronics. The entire functional array is then transferred to a target application substrate, after which the scaffold is removed. This approach leverages standard CMOS processes for the donor electronics while enabling the dense, addressable integration of nanostructures on diverse target materials that are incompatible with direct CMOS processing.

ideas.title: Develop selective contacts for nanowire devices
ideas.description: Engineer metal contacts that form ohmic or specific barrier heights to n-type and p-type nanowires.
ideas.sourceTitle: Indium phosphide nanowires as building blocks for nanoscale electronic and optoelectronic devices

ideas.title: Nanowire Capture with Optical Resonance Frequency Shift Detection
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that direct electrical readout is necessary for nanowire-based biosensors by drawing an analogy from Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) Biosensing. In SPR, biomolecular binding on a metal film is detected optically through shifts in resonance conditions, eliminating the need for direct electrical interfacing with the molecules. Adapted to this context, the nanowire array serves as a high-surface-area capture platform where each nanowire acts as an optical resonator. The binding of target analytes to functionalized nanowire surfaces alters the local refractive index, causing a measurable shift in the nanowire's optical resonance frequency. This shift can be detected using label-free optical techniques like resonant scattering microscopy or integrated photonic circuits, providing sensitive, multiplexed detection without the complexities and potential signal-to-noise issues of direct nanowire electrical measurements.

ideas.title: Low-Temperature Synthesis of Biofunctional Nanomaterials for Multiplexed Detection of Labile Neurological Biomarkers
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel low-temperature or room-temperature wet-chemical synthesis routes to produce functional nanomaterials (e.g., conductive polymers, tailored metal-organic frameworks, or composite nanoparticles) that preserve the activity of integrated, thermally sensitive biological recognition elements. These 'gentle synthesis' protocols are specifically designed to fabricate the core components of a multiplexed electrochemical or optical detection platform. The key innovation is using this platform to simultaneously quantify a panel of low-stability protein and peptide biomarkers for complex neurological (e.g., Alzheimer's, Parkinson's) and autoimmune diseases directly from biofluids, overcoming a major limitation where standard sensor manufacturing degrades the very biomarkers it aims to detect.

ideas.title: Invert the VLS Paradigm via a Solid-State Epitaxial Scaffolding Mechanism
ideas.description: This idea inverts the classic vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) growth paradigm by drawing an analogy from tissue engineering's use of degradable scaffolds. There, cells are guided to form elongated tissues by a pre-patterned solid template. Adapted to nanomaterials synthesis, the research would investigate using a pre-formed solid nanowire (e.g., a sacrificial material or a compatible crystal) as a primary growth template. The target 1D material would be grown directly onto this scaffold via solid-state epitaxial mechanisms or confined solution-phase reactions, where the scaffold's crystallography and geometry dictate the final nanowire's orientation and morphology, potentially enabling new material combinations and growth pathways not accessible through VLS.

ideas.title: Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA)-Inspired Reconfigurable Biosensor Arrays
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from digital electronics, this idea challenges the assumption of static, pre-defined marker panels by creating adaptive biosensor arrays inspired by Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs). In electronics, FPGAs use a grid of reconfigurable logic blocks and interconnects that can be programmed post-fabrication for different computational tasks. Adapting this solution, we propose a biosensing platform with a foundational grid of unspecialized, addressable micro-electrodes. Coupled with integrated microfluidics, this system can dynamically deliver and anchor different bioreceptor types (e.g., antibodies, aptamers) to specific sites on demand, effectively 'programming' the array's specificity in real-time for a given assay. This allows a single device to be reconfigured for a sequence of different diagnostic panels, moving from a fixed-function 'ASIC' model to a flexible, multi-assay 'FPGA' model for biological sensing.

ideas.title: Chromogenic Multiplexing via Engineered Reporter Cascades
ideas.description: This idea inverts the prevailing assumption that label-free detection is superior for multiplexing by intentionally employing labels to enhance capability and simplify hardware. Drawing an analogy from histopathology, where sequential chromogenic staining overcomes spectral limits for multiplex immunohistochemistry, we adapt the solution of cyclic labeling and signal erasure. The adapted research idea is to design a biosensing platform where multiplexing is achieved not through simultaneous, label-free detection of multiple analytes, but through sequential, timed detection cycles. Each target binds a dedicated, high-gain label (e.g., enzyme-linked, nanoparticle), producing a strong signal in a single, optimized readout channel (e.g., a simple photodiode for colorimetry, a basic electrode for amperometry). After measurement, a wash step chemically inactivates or enzymatically cleaves the label, resetting the sensor surface for the next cycle. This transforms the multiplexing challenge from a spatial/spectral separation problem into a temporal sequencing problem, allowing highly multiplexed detection with exceptionally simple, low-cost, and robust single-channel electronics.

ideas.title: Low-Cost, Disorder-Engineered Nanowire Sensor Platforms
ideas.description: This research direction moves beyond simply accepting imperfect materials to actively engineering and controlling disorder in low-cost, non-silicon nanowires (e.g., metal oxides, polymers, or chalcogenides) for next-generation sensors. It investigates how specific types of amorphous phases or polycrystalline grain boundary networks can be synthetically tailored to introduce beneficial electronic, optical, or chemical properties, such as trap states for enhanced gas adsorption or conductive pathways for strain detection. The goal is to establish fabrication paradigms where the low-temperature, scalable processes that produce non-crystalline structures are leveraged as a primary tool to design sensor performance, rather than a compromise, ultimately decoupling high functionality from high fabrication cost and crystalline perfection.

ideas.title: Harnessing Disordered Nanowire Networks via Glass-Ceramic Composite Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that only single-crystalline nanowires are useful by drawing an analogy from structural materials science, specifically the development of glass-ceramic composites. In that field, combining an amorphous glass phase with a polycrystalline ceramic phase creates materials with superior, tailored properties. The analogous solution involves precise control over the nucleation and growth of crystalline regions within an amorphous matrix. Adapted to nanowire research, this suggests a study focused on the deliberate synthesis and characterization of composite nanowires with engineered amorphous and polycrystalline domains. By controlling the synthesis parameters to tailor the nanocrystalline phase's size, distribution, and volume fraction within an amorphous nanowire, we can create a new class of nanomaterials. The goal is to optimize functional properties—such as electronic transport, mechanical robustness, and surface reactivity—for applications in electronics, sensors, and energy conversion, leveraging the unique synergies of the composite structure rather than seeking perfect crystalline order.

ideas.title: Integrated Microfluidic Cartridge System for Robust Point-of-Care Nanowire Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research develops a portable diagnostic device centered on a disposable, self-contained microfluidic cartridge that integrates sample preparation with the nanowire sensor array. The cartridge automates filtration, dilution, and controlled delivery of the processed sample to the sensor chip, while also containing calibration and regeneration fluids. The reusable handheld reader provides precise fluidic control, electronic readout, and data processing, ensuring laboratory-grade assay conditions are maintained at the point of care. This closed-system approach overcomes the primary failure mode of matrix interference and sensor fouling, enabling reliable use with raw biological samples by non-experts in field settings.

ideas.title: Signal Amplification via Cooperative Low-Affinity Binding Networks
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that biosensing requires highly specific, high-affinity surface receptors like antibodies. Drawing an analogy from structural biology, where the specific native structure of a protein emerges from a cooperative network of many weak, non-covalent interactions, we propose a sensing strategy based on cooperative low-affinity binding networks. The adapted solution is to develop sensor interfaces functionalized with dense, organized arrays of low-affinity or broadly reactive capture agents (e.g., engineered peptide motifs, carbohydrate clusters). When a target analyte interacts with this surface, it engages in multiple simultaneous weak interactions, creating a strong and specific avidity-based signal through cooperative binding. This approach relaxes the need for exquisitely specific receptors, potentially enabling the detection of targets that are difficult to capture with traditional high-affinity binders or improving sensor robustness against receptor degradation.

ideas.title: Closed-loop discovery of disorder-engineered transport phenomena in non-crystalline nanowires
ideas.description: This project aims to systematically overcome the performance limitations of disordered nanowires by treating their non-crystalline structure not as a defect but as a design dimension. A high-throughput robotic platform synthesizes amorphous and polycrystalline nanowire libraries with finely graded variations in composition, thermal history, and stress state. Each variant is characterized with automated nanoscale transport measurements and advanced structural analysis to build a predictive model linking synthesis parameters to disorder metrics and functional performance. The core objective is to identify and experimentally validate specific regimes where engineered disorder introduces superior or novel electronic, photonic, or ionic transport properties unattainable in perfect single crystals.

ideas.title: Dual: Assemble superlattices into 3D bulk metamaterials
ideas.description: Use synthesized superlattice nanowires as building blocks for 3D bulk materials with novel properties.
ideas.sourceTitle: Growth of nanowire superlattice structures for nanoscale photonics and electronics

ideas.title: Low-Temperature Synthesis with Targeted Post-Processing for High-Quality Material Properties
ideas.description: This approach develops hybrid synthesis routes that first use low-temperature or room-temperature methods to deposit or form a material on sensitive substrates, preserving their integrity. It then employs precisely controlled, non-thermal or localized energy inputs—like pulsed light, scanned electron beams, or chemical annealing—to selectively improve the material's microstructure and properties at the nanoscale. The system is designed to decouple the thermal budget applied to the substrate from that applied to the synthesized material itself, enabling the final product to achieve performance metrics comparable to high-temperature synthesis. This creates a viable pathway for integrating high-performance functional materials with thermally sensitive platforms like plastics, bio-scaffolds, or pre-patterned electronic devices.

ideas.title: Jointly learning sensor physics and signal sources via deep inverse modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a unified deep learning framework that treats the nanowire sensor array and the complex biomarker mixture as a single, coupled inverse problem. Instead of a sequential calibration-then-measurement process, the model is trained to jointly estimate the precise binding profile (sensitivity, specificity, cross-talk) of each sensor element and the concentrations of individual biomarkers in a sample from the raw, overlapping signal responses. This approach turns sensor non-uniformity and cross-reactivity from noise sources into informative features, enabling accurate deconvolution on imperfect, low-cost hardware. The result is a robust, calibration-light platform for multiplexed biomarker detection in complex biological fluids.

ideas.title: Kinetic Calibration: Using Enzyme Activity Profiles to Map and Correct Nanowire Sensor Heterogeneity
ideas.description: This research direction develops a method where a nanowire sensor array is exposed to a solution containing an enzyme with known kinetic behavior (e.g., a standardized telomerase or protease). The real-time, multi-site kinetic binding data from the enzyme's activity—its characteristic reaction rate and binding profile—is used as a dynamic 'fingerprint' to solve the inverse problem and map the functional heterogeneity (e.g., variations in sensitivity, binding affinity, and response time) across the sensor surface. Once characterized, this spatial map is used to correct the raw signal from subsequent assays of unknown enzyme samples, transforming the array from a collection of inconsistent sensors into a quantitatively reliable platform for comparing subtle kinetic differences in enzyme function, crucial for diagnostic applications like inhibitor screening or detecting pathological enzyme variants.

ideas.title: Sensor Surface Calibration via Biomarker Tomography
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the inverse problem in medical computed tomography (CT) to the context of nanowire sensor arrays. In CT, an object's internal structure is reconstructed from multiple X-ray projections. Here, a known panel of biomarkers with distinct binding characteristics is used as the 'projection' sources. By applying these biomarkers in specific sequences or spatial patterns to the sensor array, their measured binding profiles collectively form a dataset. Solving the corresponding inverse problem with tomographic reconstruction algorithms allows for the detailed characterization and calibration of the non-uniformities in the sensor surface's sensitivity and binding properties, effectively creating a functional map for subsequent quantitative measurements.

ideas.title: Adaptive Signal Processing for Imperfect Nanomaterial Sensors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that high-performance sensors require pristine, defect-free silicon nanowires by drawing an analogy from wireless communications. In that field, robust systems are built not by creating perfect transmission channels, but by developing adaptive signal processing that works reliably with noisy, imperfect ones. The adapted research idea proposes to apply this principle to sensors made from non-silicon or structurally imperfect nanomaterials. The solution is to co-design low-cost, tolerant nanomaterial fabrication processes with intelligent sensor readout electronics and algorithms. These algorithms would be trained to characterize and compensate for the specific non-idealities (e.g., baseline drift, heterogeneity in response) of a given sensor or batch, transforming a cheaper, imperfect material into a reliable sensing platform through computational correction, rather than purely material-level perfection.

ideas.title: Closed-loop self-correcting microfluidics with integrated fouling mitigation for robust nanowire array automation
ideas.description: This system integrates microfluidic automation with real-time diagnostic and corrective functions to overcome the critical failure mode of clogging and fouling. A network of embedded sensors continuously monitors fluidic integrity, triggering targeted countermeasures like acoustic agitation or localized backflush when anomalies are detected. This design ensures highly reliable, long-term automated sample handling and washing for sensitive nanowire array biosensors. The approach transforms the microfluidic component from a passive conduit into an actively managed, fault-tolerant subsystem.

ideas.title: A Multiplexed Immunoassay Panel for Early-Stage, Subtype-Specific Diagnosis of Neurodegenerative Disorders
ideas.description: This research seed proposes applying a multiplexed detection platform to biomarkers for complex non-cancer diseases, specifically neurological disorders. By drawing an analogy from environmental science, where sensor arrays are used to fingerprint and deconvolute complex mixtures of pollutants, we adapt this solution to clinical diagnostics. The adapted idea is to create a multiplexed immunoassay panel designed to detect a suite of protein biomarkers in CSF and blood, calibrating it to generate distinct biosignatures for major neurodegenerative diseases. This approach aims to enable early-stage, subtype-specific diagnosis from a single sample, addressing a critical unmet need in neurology by providing a tool more capable of handling the complex, overlapping pathology of these disorders than single-analyte tests.

ideas.title: A Scaling Law Approach to Quantum Confinement in Nanowires
ideas.description: This research adapts the scaling law approach from fluid dynamics to systematically investigate quantum confinement effects in nanowires. The analogous problem of predicting aerodynamic properties across different scales and conditions is solved using dimensionless numbers and scaling relations. The adapted idea is to develop a universal theoretical framework centered on a key dimensionless confinement parameter (e.g., diameter/Bohr radius) that governs shifts in electronic and optical properties. By establishing master scaling laws, the behavior of diverse nanowire materials (e.g., Si, GaAs, CdSe) under diameter reduction can be predicted and organized onto unified curves. This approach would be validated through targeted synthesis and characterization of a strategically chosen subset of materials, moving beyond case-by-case studies to a predictive, generalizable understanding of nanoscale confinement.

ideas.title: Defect-Engineered Solid-State Templating for Single-Crystal One-Dimensional Growth
ideas.description: This adversarially improved research idea proposes a hybrid pathway that leverages the diameter control and material versatility of solid-state or solution-phase templating while directly solving its fatal flaw of poor crystallinity. The core innovation is a defect-targeted, post-nucleation annealing protocol guided by in-situ characterization to transform initially polycrystalline or defective nanowire precursors into single-crystalline wires. The system is designed to decouple the diameter-defining step (template formation) from the crystallinity-defining step (defect annihilation), enabling the synthesis of one-dimensional materials with both the structural precision of VLS and the substrate compatibility and scalability of solid-state approaches.

ideas.title: Computationally Adaptive Sensing via Post-Measurement Virtual Panel Extraction
ideas.description: This system utilizes a fixed, highly multiplexed array capable of simultaneously capturing a vast, predefined set of molecular signals in a single measurement. The adaptability is transferred from the physical hardware to the computational analysis layer. After data acquisition, user-defined or algorithmically generated 'virtual panels' are applied to the rich dataset to isolate and quantify only the target markers for a specific assay. This approach eliminates the speed, reliability, and calibration bottlenecks of physical reconfiguration while providing ultimate flexibility to redefine assays post-hoc without ever re-running the sample.

ideas.title: Graded-Index Nanowires via Controlled Vapor-Liquid-Solid Tapering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that nanowire diameter must be uniform for optimal function. Drawing an analogy from fiber optics, where graded-index fibers solved the problem of modal dispersion by creating a smooth gradient in refractive index, we propose adapting this solution to nanowire synthesis. The analogous solution is implemented by dynamically modulating precursor flow, temperature, or catalyst composition during VLS growth to create nanowires with a pre-designed, non-uniform diameter profile. This adapted approach allows for the creation of 'graded-index nanowires' where properties like bandgap, carrier mobility, and mechanical strain vary continuously along the axis, opening applications in gradient-index waveguides, broadband photon harvesting, and strain-engineered electronic devices that were impossible with uniform-diameter wires.

ideas.title: Programmable Diameter Gradients via Solid-State or Solution-Phase Templated Growth
ideas.description: This research direction seeks to develop fundamentally new synthesis pathways for one-dimensional nanomaterials with intentionally programmed diameter variations along their axis. By moving beyond the vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) paradigm to solid-state (e.g., stress-driven extrusion, corrosion) or solution-phase (e.g., directional electrodeposition, confined precipitation) growth mechanisms, we gain direct spatial and temporal control over the local growth kinetics. This control allows for the dynamic modulation of the growth front's geometry or the local chemical environment to create precise diameter gradients, steps, or periodic features in situ. The resulting nanostructures would possess continuous property gradients (e.g., bandgap, mechanical stiffness, catalytic activity) tailored for advanced applications in graded-index photonics, strain-graded composites, or electrodes with optimized mass transport.

ideas.title: Active Biofouling-Management System for Long-Term Implantable Biomarker Sensors
ideas.description: This research develops an implantable biomarker sensor with an integrated, active system to prevent biofouling and ensure long-term accuracy. The core innovation is a multi-strategy anti-fouling platform that dynamically maintains a clean sensor surface through physical, chemical, and electrochemical means. A micro-controller coordinates these cycles, optimizing power use to enable continuous, real-time monitoring for extended periods. This approach directly targets the primary cause of implantable sensor failure, aiming to transform them from short-term diagnostic tools into reliable chronic disease management systems.

ideas.title: Transient Non-Metallic Catalytic Templating for Contamination-Free Nanowire Growth
ideas.description: This research develops a catalyst-free growth paradigm by employing a transient, removable template to direct nanowire nucleation and anisotropic growth without leaving behind metal impurities. The system utilizes non-metallic, volatile, or soluble nanoparticles (e.g., alkali halides, organic crystals) that act as a temporary catalytic phase under specific growth conditions, mimicking the VLS mechanism. After single-crystalline nanowire growth is complete, the template is completely removed via a gentle, selective process, yielding ultra-clean semiconductor nanowires. This approach directly addresses the material quality shortcomings of purely catalyst-free methods while achieving the original goal of eliminating metallic contamination.

ideas.title: Catalyst-Free Nanowire Growth via Photochemical Initiation Inspired by Polymerization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that metal catalysts are necessary for the anisotropic growth of semiconductor nanowires by drawing an analogy from polymer chemistry. In that field, controlled radical polymerization techniques like photo-ATRP or RAFT achieve precise, chain-length-controlled polymer synthesis using purely organic photoinitiators or chain transfer agents, eliminating metal catalysts to prevent contamination. The adapted research idea proposes to translate this photochemical initiation and control strategy to chemical vapor deposition (CVD) systems for nanowires. By using tailored ultraviolet or visible light irradiation on a patterned, non-catalytic substrate (e.g., a dielectric with defined nucleation sites), the goal is to selectively decompose semiconductor precursors and drive directional, catalyst-free nanowire growth via a photochemically activated vapor-solid mechanism, thereby completely avoiding metal contamination.

ideas.title: Orthogonal Validation Gates for High-Fidelity Sensing with Broadly Reactive Capture Agents
ideas.description: This approach enables the use of low-affinity, broadly reactive capture elements by incorporating a subsequent, mandatory validation step that confers high specificity. The system first captures a wide range of potential targets via generic interactions (e.g., hydrophobic patches, common glycan motifs). A positive signal is only generated if the captured entity also triggers a secondary, orthogonal transducer designed to recognize a distinct, target-specific feature not used in the initial capture, such as a unique enzymatic activity or a precise spatial arrangement of epitopes. This sequential gating strategy maintains the advantage of broad capture while eliminating false positives, making it viable for direct analysis in complex biological or environmental fluids.
